title: Culex
author: Appendix Vergiliana
classification_notes: The 'Culex' is a poem in the Appendix Vergiliana, traditionally attributed to Virgil but of disputed authorship. It is a common work within the study of Virgil and early imperial Latin poetry, often read for its literary qualities and its relation to the Virgilian corpus.

author: Appendix Vergiliana
classification_notes: De Est et Non is a short didactic poem from the Appendix Vergiliana, a collection of works traditionally but doubtfully attributed to Virgil. Its subject is the grammatical use of 'est' and 'non' in Latin, and it is of interest primarily to scholars of pseudo-Virgilian poetry and ancient grammatical verse.

author: Appendix Vergiliana

title: Dirae
author: Appendix Vergiliana

title: De Deo Socratis
author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius' De Deo Socratis, a philosophical work on Socrates' daemon, blends Platonism with Roman religious thought. It is a key text for Middle Platonism and Apuleius' philosophical oeuvre.

author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius's De Deo Socratis is a philosophical speech on the daimon of Socrates. It blends Platonic philosophy with rhetorical flair and is an important work for understanding Middle Platonism and Apuleius's thought.

title: De Mundo
author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius' De Mundo is a Latin translation and adaptation of the pseudo-Aristotelian Greek treatise On the Cosmos. It reflects the Middle Platonic interest in cosmology and the harmony of the universe. As a philosophical work by Apuleius, best known for his novel The Golden Ass, De Mundo is a common text for those studying the Latin philosophical tradition and the reception of Greek Peripatetic ideas in the Roman Empire.

title: De Platone et Eius Dogmate
author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius' De Platone et Eius Dogmate is a philosophical treatise expounding Platonic doctrine, part of his broader philosophical corpus. It is commonly read by students of Middle Platonism and Latin philosophical prose.

title: Florida
author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius's Florida is a collection of rhetorical excerpts and speeches showcasing his ornate and witty Latin style. The work provides valuable insight into the Second Sophistic and Latin rhetoric of the Antonine period. It is a text of common interest within the study of later Latin literature and rhetoric.

title: Metamorphoses
author: Apuleius
classification_notes: Apuleius' Metamorphoses (also known as The Golden Ass) is the only Latin novel to survive in its entirety. It is a picaresque narrative blending comedy, fantasy, and religious allegory, with the famous tale of Cupid and Psyche embedded within. The work is a major text in the Latin literary canon, widely read and studied for its linguistic innovation, narrative structure, and cultural significance.

source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Maecenas, Gaius Cilnius ca. 70 B.C.-8 B.C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of poetry by C. Maecenas, the famous patron of the arts. His own literary output was limited, and these fragments are rare witnesses to his poetic style.

title: fragmentum a Morel omissum
author: C. Maecenas
classification_notes: This fragmentum a Morel omissum by C. Maecenas is a tiny 4-word fragment omitted from standard collections. It is an extremely obscure text, of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Latin poetry and textual transmission.

title: orationes
author: C. Memmius
classification_notes: C. Memmius' oration fragment.

title: carmina
author: C. Memmius L. f.
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Memmius, C ca. 98-ca. 46 B.C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Only 7 words survive from the carmina of C. Memmius L. f., a Roman politician and patron of literature known as the dedicatee of Lucretius' De Rerum Natura. These fragments, preserved in the Fragmenta Poetarum Latinorum, are too scant for literary analysis but offer a tantalizing glimpse into the poetic activity of a Late Republican figure. The work is obscure, known only to specialists in Latin poetic fragments.

title: orationes
author: C. Memmius L. f.
classification_notes: These fragments of speeches by C. Memmius L. f., a Roman politician and orator of the late Republic, amount to only 45 words. The fragments are too slight to offer insight into his rhetorical style. They are of minimal interest except to specialists in the fragmentary remains of Roman oratory.

title: De Silvestribus Arboribus
author: C. Oppius
classification_notes: A fragment of C. Oppius's De Silvestribus Arboribus, a Latin technical treatise on woodland trees. This work belongs to the Roman tradition of agricultural and botanical writing.

title: vitae
author: C. Oppius
classification_notes: C. Oppius' vitae (biographies) survive in only 10 words, making them virtually unrecoverable. Known only through later testimonia, they are a marginal relic of Late Republican biographical writing.

title: oratio
author: C. Papirius Carbo
classification_notes: A 23-word fragment of a speech by C. Papirius Carbo, an orator and tribune of the late Republic. The brief fragment provides slight evidence of his rhetorical style.

title: oratio
author: C. Papirius Carbo Arvina
classification_notes: A fragment of a speech (oratio) by C. Papirius Carbo Arvina, an orator of the late Republic. Only 30 words survive, providing limited evidence of his rhetorical style.

title: oratio
author: C. Scribonius Curio avus
classification_notes: This oration by C. Scribonius Curio avus survives only in a brief fragment of 15 words. As a minor piece of late Republican oratory, it is of interest only to specialists in Roman rhetoric and fragmentary speech traditions.

title: orationes
author: C. Scribonius Curio pater
classification_notes: A single fragment of a speech by C. Scribonius Curio pater. Almost nothing of his oratory survives; the text is too brief to assess his rhetorical style.

title: orationes
author: C. Sempronius Gracchus
classification_notes: Fragments of speeches by C. Sempronius Gracchus, the prominent Roman tribune and orator. These fragments capture his passionate style and political reforms.

title: orationes
author: C. Servilius Glaucia
classification_notes: These orationes by Servilius Glaucia survive only in fragments. They are of obscure interest, known only from testimonia.

title: oratio
author: C. Titius
classification_notes: C. Titius' oratio survives only in a fragment. As an early Roman orator, his work is of specialist interest for the history of Latin rhetoric and oratory.

title: iurisprudentia et al.
author: C. Trebatius Testa
classification_notes: These fragments of 'iurisprudentia et al.' by C. Trebatius Testa are remnants of his legal writings. Trebatius was a prominent jurist, and these fragments are of specialist interest for Roman legal history.

title: carmina
author: C. Valgius Rufus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Valgius Rufus, C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of poems by Valgius Rufus, an Augustan poet and friend of Horace, preserved in later citations. The remnants are too few to assess his work fully, but they are of interest to scholars of Augustan poetry and fragmentary verse.

title: oratio
author: C. et L. Caepasii fratres
classification_notes: The 'oratio' of the Caepasii brothers is a fragmentary speech, known only from a citation. The brothers are minor figures in Roman oratory, and the work is of interest only to specialists in Republican rhetoric.

title: versus in Naevium
author: Caecilius Metellus
classification_notes: Caecilius Metellus' verse against Naevius is a tiny fragment of early Latin satire, preserved only in quotation.

title: palliatae
author: Caecilius Statius
classification_notes: Caecilius Statius' palliatae are fragments of his Roman comedies adapted from Greek New Comedy. As a significant but fragmentary comic playwright, his work is of specialist interest to scholars of Roman drama and literary history.

title: Brevis Ciborum, excerpta
author: Caelius Apicius
classification_notes: This excerpt of Apicius's recipe collection, De Re Coquinaria, is a rare survival of Roman culinary literature, offering insight into imperial gastronomy.

title: Brevis Pimentorum, excerpta
author: Caelius Apicius
classification_notes: The Brevis Pimentorum, excerpta is a short excerpt from Apicius' cookbook, listing spices (pimenta). Apicius is the major surviving Roman cookery author. This excerpt (132 words) is a fragment of the larger De Re Coquinaria. It is of specialist interest for food history and Roman pharmacology.

title: De Re Coquinaria
author: Caelius Apicius
classification_notes: Apicius's De Re Coquinaria, a collection of Roman recipes and the only surviving cookbook from antiquity.

title: E Parmenide de natura
author: Caelius Aurelianus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Aurelianus, Caelius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Caelius Aurelianus' E Parmenide de natura is a fragmentary philosophical text, likely a Latin translation or adaptation of a Greek work. It is an obscure specialist text, of interest primarily to historians of late antique philosophy and medicine.

title: grammatica, fragmenta
author: Caesellius Vindex
classification_notes: Caesellius Vindex's grammatical work survives only in fragments. He was a Roman grammarian of the 2nd century CE, cited by later writers on Latin language.

title: De Statu Rei Publicae sp.
author: Caleni uxor Sulpicia
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Sulpicia; perseus_editor=Peiper, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1886; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The fragment 'De Statu Rei Publicae' attributed to Sulpicia, wife of Calenus, is a didactic poem on the state of the republic. Sulpicia is one of the few known female Latin poets of the early Empire. This fragment offers insight into women's literary production.

title: carmen
author: Caleni uxor Sulpicia
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Sulpicia; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A single short poem attributed to Sulpicia (Caleni uxor), preserved in Morel's anthology. This fragment provides a rare female voice in Latin poetry from the Augustan period, of specialist interest for gender studies and the history of women in Roman literature.

title: Declamationes, excerpta
author: Calpurnius Flaccus
classification_notes: Calpurnius Flaccus' Declamationes, excerpta are a collection of rhetorical exercises (declamations) surviving only as excerpts. They are a key source for the study of Roman declamation and rhetorical education in the Imperial period.

author: Canon
classification_notes: A substantial anonymous Latin work possibly dealing with a canonical list, of uncertain content.

title: Carmen Arvale
author: Carmen Arvale
classification_notes: The 'Carmen Arvale' is a fragmentary archaic Latin hymn, preserved in an inscription. It is a rare and important witness to early Roman religion and language, of specialist interest to scholars of archaic Latin and Roman ritual.

title: Carmen Devotionis
author: Carmen Devotionis
classification_notes: Carmen Devotionis is a fragmentary hymn or prayer. It is of obscure interest, surviving only in brief excerpts.

title: Carmen Evocationis
author: Carmen Evocationis
classification_notes: The Carmen Evocationis is a fragmentary Roman ritual formula for summoning deities. It is of specialist interest for the study of Roman religion, ritual language, and archaic Latin.

title: Carmen de Bello Aegyptiaco
author: Carmen de Bello Aegyptiaco
classification_notes: The Carmen de Bello Aegyptiaco is an anonymous epic poem about the war in Egypt, likely the Battle of Actium. It is a rare example of historical epic from the early Empire and is of interest for its content and unknown authorship.

title: carmina
author: Catullus
classification_notes: Catullus' Carmina is a major collection of lyric and epigrammatic poetry, widely read for its emotional intensity and literary innovation. It is a canonical work of Latin poetry, central to the study of Roman literature.

title: carminum fragmenta
author: Catullus
classification_notes: Fragments of Catullus' poems, very short, providing minor additions to his corpus.

title: Ex Graecis Conversiones
author: Chalcidius

classification_notes: Chalcidius' Ex Graecis Conversiones is a fragmentary Latin translation of Greek philosophical texts, likely related to his commentary on Plato's Timaeus. It is of interest to scholars of late-antique Platonism and translation.

title: Aratea
author: Claudius Caesar Germanicus
classification_notes: Germanicus' 'Aratea' is a Latin translation/adaptation of Aratus' 'Phaenomena', a didactic poem on astronomy and weather signs. It is a key text for the study of Latin didactic poetry and the reception of Hellenistic science.

title: epigrammata
author: Claudius Caesar Germanicus
classification_notes: Germanicus' 'epigrammata' are fragmentary poems attributed to the Roman general and imperial prince. They are of interest to specialists in imperial Latin poetry and the literary activities of the Julio-Claudian family.

title: fragmenta Aratea
author: Claudius Caesar Germanicus
classification_notes: Germanicus' Fragmenta Aratea are fragments of his Latin translation/adaptation of Aratus' Phaenomena, a didactic poem on astronomy. The work is an important example of Latin scientific poetry. The fragments (1,420 words) are incomplete but valuable for the study of early imperial astronomy and Latin verse translation.

title: grammatica fragmenta

classification_notes: Fragments of grammatical works by Cloatius Verus, an otherwise obscure Roman grammarian. The text survives only in citations and is of minor importance for the history of Latin grammar.

title: grammatica, fragmenta
author: Clodius Tuscus
classification_notes: Clodius Tuscus' grammatical work survives only in a few fragments. Little is known of the author, and the text is too fragmentary to inform broader grammatical studies beyond its testimonium value.

title: iurisprudentia, fragmenta
author: Cn. Arulenus Caelius Sabinus
classification_notes: Fragments of 'iurisprudentia' from the jurist Cn. Arulenus Caelius Sabinus. These 124 words are a specialist resource for Roman legal history and the reconstruction of its jurisprudential literature.

title: carmen

classification_notes: Lentulus Gaetulicus' carmen fragment.

title: orationes

classification_notes: These orationes by Lentulus Marcellinus survive only in fragments, known from quotations. They are of obscure interest, preserved only in testimonia.

title: oratio
author: Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus
classification_notes: Domitius Ahenobarbus's oration survives only in a tiny fragment, attesting to his role in Republican political life.

title: Annales

classification_notes: A 113-word fragment of Cn. Gellius' Annales, a historical work of the Roman Republic. The text survives only through later quotations and is valuable for the study of early Roman annalistic historiography.

title: praecepta

classification_notes: The 'praecepta' of Cn. Marcius vates, a 16-word fragment, belongs to a shadowy figure of early Roman prophetic or poetic tradition. Its content and genre are highly uncertain, placing it among the most obscure survivals of archaic Latin literature.

title: carmina
author: Cn. Matius

classification_notes: Fragments of Cn. Matius' carmina.

title: Bellum Punicum
author: Cn. Naevius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin | Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Naevius, Gnaeus ca. 270-ca. 200 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino | Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1907 | 1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Naevius' Bellum Punicum, an epic poem on the First Punic War, survives only in fragments. It is a foundational text for Latin epic, influential on Ennius and Virgil, and important for early Roman literature.

title: alia carmina epica
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: The fragmentary 'alia carmina epica' of Cn. Naevius represent additional epic material outside his Bellum Poenicum. Naevius was a pioneering Roman epic and dramatic poet active in the 3rd century BCE. These fragments, though brief, contribute to our understanding of early Latin epic tradition.

title: carmina, frr. a Morel omissa
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: These fragments of Naevius's poems, omitted by Morel, are tiny remnants of his poetic output. They provide scant evidence for his work and are of interest primarily to specialists in early Latin poetry.

title: palliatae
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: Fragments of Naevius's palliatae (comedies in Greek dress), an important early Roman dramatist.

title: praetextae
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: Naevius's praetextae are fragments of historical dramas based on Roman themes. These are among the earliest examples of Roman tragedy and provide insight into the development of Latin drama.

title: tragoediae
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: The tragedies of Cn. Naevius, an early Roman playwright, survive in fragments that show his innovation in adapting Greek models to Roman themes (including fabulae praetextae). Despite loss, they are a commonly studied source for the development of Roman drama and the Punic Wars period.

title: versus in Metellos sp.
author: Cn. Naevius
classification_notes: A tiny fragment of Naevius' verse against the Metelli. It is one of the earliest surviving pieces of Latin literature and reflects early political satire in Saturnian meter.

title: de re rustica
author: Cn. Tremelius Scrofa
classification_notes: This exiguous fragment of Tremelius Scrofa's work on agriculture, preserved perhaps through Varro, is a rare witness to Roman agronomic writing.

title: Commentarii Augurum
author: Commentarii Augurum
classification_notes: The 'Commentarii Augurum' are records of the Roman college of augurs, preserved only in fragments. They are a specialist source for Roman state religion and ritual procedure, of interest primarily to scholars of Roman religion and law.

title: Commentarii Consulares
author: Commentarii Consulares
classification_notes: Commentarii Consulares, fragmentary records of consular years, of uncertain authorship.

title: Comment. Anquisit. Sergii
author: Commentarius Anquisitionis M'. Sergii M'. f. Quaestoris
classification_notes: The Commentarius Anquisitionis of M'. Sergius, a quaestor's legal commentary. This 130-word fragment concerns the inquistion procedure, a part of Roman criminal law.

title: grammatica
author: Cornelius Epicadus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Cornelius Epicadus; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cornelius Epicadus's grammatical work survives only in a fragment of seven words. The content and scope are almost entirely lost, making it a text of minimal scholarly impact.

title: Vitae
author: Cornelius Nepos
classification_notes: Cornelius Nepos' 'Vitae' is a collection of biographies of famous commanders and historians. It is a key work in the development of Latin biography.

title: fragmenta
author: Cornelius Nepos
classification_notes: Cornelius Nepos' fragmenta are remnants of his biographical and historical works, including the 'De Viris Illustribus'. They provide glimpses into his corpus, which was important for Latin biography and historical writing.

title: carmina
author: Cornelius Severus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Severus, Cornelius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The poems (carmina) of Cornelius Severus, an Augustan epic poet, are preserved in fragments. His historical epic on Roman themes (possibly on the Sicilian War or Caesar) attracted praise from later critics. These fragments are of specialist interest for the development of Roman historical epic.

title: fragmenta a Morel omissa
author: Cornelius Severus
classification_notes: These fragments omitted by Morel and attributed to Cornelius Severus are among the most obscure remnants of Latin literature. The total word count is only 9 words, providing virtually no context for the original work. The nature of the fragments (likely epic or historical poetry) is inferred from the author's known association with epic, but the classification remains highly uncertain.

title: Annales
author: Cornelius Tacitus
classification_notes: Tacitus's Annales is a masterwork of Roman historiography, chronicling the Julio-Claudian dynasty with penetrating analysis of power and politics.

title: De Origine et Situ Germanorum
author: Cornelius Tacitus
classification_notes: Tacitus' De Origine et Situ Germanorum (Germania) is an ethnographic monograph describing the geography, customs, and tribes of Germania. It is a foundational work for the study of Roman ethnography, ancient Germanic peoples, and Tacitean historiography.

title: De Vita Iulii Agricolae
author: Cornelius Tacitus
classification_notes: Tacitus's Agricola, a biography of his father-in-law Gnaeus Julius Agricola, recounting his military campaigns in Britain. It is a key historical source for Roman Britain and a masterpiece of Latin prose.

title: Dialogus de Oratoribus
author: Cornelius Tacitus
classification_notes: Tacitus' 'Dialogus de Oratoribus' is a major work on the decline of Roman oratory, set in the Flavian period. It is central to the study of Latin rhetoric, literary history, and Tacitus' oeuvre.

title: Historiae
author: Cornelius Tacitus
classification_notes: Tacitus' Histories, a seminal work of Roman historiography covering the Year of the Four Emperors and the Flavian dynasty. Its dense style and political analysis make it a cornerstone of Latin literature.

title: versus in Vergilium
author: Cornificius Gallus
classification_notes: Cornificius Gallus's verse against Vergil is a slight epigrammatic attack, preserved as a fragment. Its interest lies primarily in the context of literary rivalries in the Augustan period, but the text is too minimal for broad literary significance.

title: grammatica, fragmenta
author: Cornificius Longus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Cornificius Longus active 44 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cornificius Longus' 'grammatica, fragmenta' are fragmentary grammatical remains. The title is generic, but the author is known as a grammarian. The work is of minimal interest outside the history of Latin linguistics.

title: versio Latina Magonis
author: D. Iunius Silanus
classification_notes: This fragment preserves a Latin translation of Mago's Carthaginian agricultural treatise, attributed to D. Iunius Silanus. Extant in only 136 words, it is a rare witness to the transmission of Phoenician agricultural expertise into Roman culture. It is of specialist interest for Roman agriculture and lost technical literature.

title: mimi
author: D. Laberius
classification_notes: The mime fragments of Decimus Laberius, a Roman knight and writer of mimes, are preserved in quotations. Laberius was known for his wit and social satire. His works offer a window into popular Roman entertainment.

title: Amphitruo
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The didascaliae and argumenta for Plautus' Amphitruo are brief scholarly prefaces summarizing the play's production and plot. They are of specialist interest for Plautine scholarship and ancient dramaturgy.

title: Asinaria
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This didascalia for Plautus' Asinaria is a brief scholarly note on the play's production. As part of the Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum, it is a specialist resource for Plautine scholarship and Roman theatrical history.

title: Aulularia
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This is a set of ancient didascaliae (production notes) and arguments (summaries) for Plautus's play Aulularia. These prefatory texts provide crucial information about the date, staging, and plot of the comedy. They are of specialist importance for scholars of Roman theater and the transmission of Plautus.

title: Captivi
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: Didascalia and argumentum for Plautus's Captivi provide a synopsis and production history of the play, aiding in the understanding of Roman comedy.

title: Casina
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The didascalia (production notice) and argumentum for Plautus' Casina is a very brief note (53 words) on the play's performance history. It is a minor source for Roman theatrical records.

title: Cistellaria
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The didascaliae and argumenta for Plautus's Cistellaria provide brief production notes and plot summaries, valuable for understanding Roman comedy's performance context.

title: Curculio
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: A short argumentum for Plautus' Curculio from the Didascaliae et Argumenta. It provides plot summary and performance context for this comedy, valuable for Plautine scholarship.

title: Epidicus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: Didascalia and argumentum for Plautus's Epidicus provide a synopsis and production history, valuable for Plautine scholarship.

title: Menaechmi
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: A short argumentum for Plautus' Menaechmi from the Didascaliae et Argumenta collection. It provides plot summary and performance details, serving as a secondary source for Plautine scholarship.

title: Mercator
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This didascalia for Plautus' Mercator is a brief scholarly note on the play's production history. As part of the Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum, it is a specialist resource for Plautine scholarship and Roman theatrical history.

title: Miles Gloriosus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This didascalia and argument for Plautus's 'Miles Gloriosus' provides a brief synopsis and production details. It is part of the paratextual tradition surrounding Plautus's comedies, likely compiled in late antiquity.

title: Mostellaria
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The 'Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum' are production notes and plot summaries for Plautus' plays. This entry for 'Mostellaria' is a brief paratextual fragment, of interest to specialists in Plautine drama and Roman theatrical history.

title: Persa
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The 'Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum' are production notes and plot summaries for Plautus' plays. This entry for 'Persa' is a brief paratextual fragment, of interest to specialists in Plautine drama and Roman theatrical history.

author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: Didascalia for Plautus' Poenulus, a production notice transmitted in the Plautine corpus.

title: Pseudolus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This 'Pseudolus' is a didascalia (production notice) and argumentum (summary) for Plautus's play. It is a specialist text, of interest to scholars of Roman theater and textual transmission.

title: Rudens
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: The 'Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum' are production notes and plot summaries for Plautus' plays. This entry for 'Rudens' is a brief paratextual fragment, of interest to specialists in Plautine drama and Roman theatrical history.

title: Stichus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This didascalia and argument for Plautus's 'Stichus' provides a brief synopsis. It is part of the paratextual tradition and helps date and contextualize the play.

title: Trinummus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: Didascaliae and argumenta for Plautus' Trinummus.

title: Truculentus
author: Didascaliae et Argumenta in Plautum
classification_notes: This is the didascalia (production note) and argumentum (summary) for Plautus' Truculentus, a part of the later paratextual tradition surrounding the plays of Plautus. With only 97 words, it is a minimal text providing the didascalic preface. It is of interest only to specialists in Plautine transmission and the history of classical scholarship.

title: Adelphoe

While not a major independent work, they serve as a valuable resource for understanding Terentian drama and reception. The text is brief and its authorship uncertain, but it was likely composed in Late Antiquity for educational purposes.

title: Andria
author: Didascaliae et Periochae in Terentium
classification_notes: The didascaliae and periochae for Terence's 'Andria' are scholarly prefaces and plot summaries that accompany the manuscript tradition. They provide essential information on Terentian production and reception, and are a standard reference for textual criticism and theatrical history.

title: Eunuchus

title: Heauton Timorumenos

title: Hecyra
author: Didascaliae et Periochae in Terentium
classification_notes: The didascalia and periocha for Terence's Hecyra is a brief note (205 words) on the play's production. It is a minor source for Roman theatrical history and the reception of Terence.

title: Phormio
author: Didascaliae et Periochae in Terentium
classification_notes: The 'Didascaliae et Periochae in Terentium' are production notes and plot summaries for Terence's plays. This entry for 'Phormio' is a brief paratextual fragment, of interest to specialists in Terentian drama and Roman theatrical history.

classification_notes: Habakkuk in the Revised Standard Version Latin text is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. Though brief, it contains the important statement 'the righteous shall live by faith', central to Pauline theology.

title: Haggai
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Haggai is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible, focusing on the rebuilding of the Temple. This Latin version derives from the RSV text. It is a short book but important for post-exilic history.

title: Hebrews
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Hebrews is a New Testament text of uncertain authorship, traditionally attributed to Paul but now widely considered anonymous. It is a sophisticated theological work emphasizing Christ's priesthood and sacrifice.

title: Hosea
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Hosea, in the RSV translation, is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible that uses the metaphor of a marital relationship to depict God's covenant with Israel. It is a foundational text for biblical prophecy and theology.

title: Isaiah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Isaiah from the Revised Standard Version of the English Bible is a major prophetic book of the Old Testament. The RSV is a widely used modern translation, and Isaiah is central to biblical studies and theology.

title: James
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Epistle of James, here in a Latin translation, is a canonical New Testament book traditionally attributed to James, the brother of Jesus. It is a short didactic letter emphasizing faith and works. In Latin, it forms part of the Vulgate tradition and has been widely read across Christian history.

title: Jeremiah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Jeremiah is a major prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible, attributed to the prophet Jeremiah. It contains prophecies of judgment, laments, and promises of restoration, and is significant for its theological depth and poetic richness.

title: Job
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Job from the Revised Standard Version, a poetic and philosophical book of the Hebrew Bible.

title: Joel
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Joel is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible. This Latin version comes from the RSV textual tradition, reflecting a modern critical edition. It is a brief work on locusts and the Day of the Lord.

title: John
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Gospel of John in the Revised Standard Version is a central New Testament text, distinguished by its theological depth and unique portrayal of Jesus. Its prologue, discourses, and passion narrative have profoundly shaped Christian thought and are extensively studied in biblical scholarship.

title: Jonah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The book of Jonah from the RSV English Bible is a short prophetic narrative about the reluctant prophet swallowed by a great fish. In a Latin corpus this is likely a bilingual or reference entry. It is a well-known biblical story, commonly read in religious and literary contexts, but less central to Latin studies.

title: Joshua
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Joshua from the Revised Standard Version, describing the conquest of Canaan.

title: Jude
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Latin representation of the Epistle of Jude, derived from the RSV tradition. Its inclusion in a Latin corpus is likely a cataloging anomaly; the work itself is a brief NT letter.

title: Judges
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Judges from the Hebrew Bible is a major historical and religious text. Its Latin versions have been central to Christian scripture and liturgy, ensuring wide and continuous readership.

title: Judith
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Judith is a deuterocanonical book of the Bible, telling the story of Judith's deliverance of Israel. This Latin version from the RSV tradition is based on the Septuagint. It is a significant work of Jewish historical fiction.

title: Lamentations
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Lamentations, a poetic book of the Old Testament in the Revised Standard Version, mourns the destruction of Jerusalem. It is a key text for Jewish and Christian liturgy and theology, and for the study of ancient Hebrew poetry. It is widely studied within biblical studies.

title: Leviticus
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Leviticus, the third book of the Torah, is presented here in the Revised Standard Version. It is a central text detailing the priestly code and laws of ritual purity. It holds canonical significance in both Jewish and Christian traditions, and is a key source for the study of ancient Israelite religion.

title: Luke
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Gospel of Luke, a central narrative of the life and teachings of Jesus, is a cornerstone of Christian scripture. This Latin record for an English translation (RSV) represents the work in its modern English form. The considerable length (25,507 words) underscores its comprehensive scope as a historical and theological biography.

title: Malachi
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Malachi from the RSV (in Latin version) is the last book of the Old Testament prophets, focusing on covenant faithfulness. It is a canonical prophetic text, widely read in Christian liturgical and exegetical traditions.

title: Mark
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Gospel of Mark from the English Revised Standard Version is a foundational Christian text. While the RSV is a modern English translation, its Latin textual basis and scholarly apparatus make it relevant for Latin biblical studies.

title: Matthew
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Latin version of the Gospel of Matthew from the RSV tradition. Despite the questionable provenance, the Gospel itself is central to the New Testament.

title: Micah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Micah from the RSV is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible. Its themes of justice and peace are widely read in biblical studies and religious contexts.

title: Nahum
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Nahum from the Revised Standard Version, a minor prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible.

title: Nehemiah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Nehemiah is a historical book in the RSV, recounting the rebuilding of Jerusalem. It is of specialist interest within biblical studies, less widely read than the Torah or Prophets.

title: Numbers
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Numbers is a key historical and legal book of the Hebrew Bible, detailing the Israelites' wilderness wanderings. This Latin version comes from the RSV tradition, based on the Masoretic Text.

title: Philippians
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Paul's Epistle to the Philippians is a letter of joy and encouragement, written from prison. It is a relatively short Pauline epistle, valued for its warmth and theological content.

title: Prayer of Azariah (Song of the Three Young Men)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Prayer of Azariah (Song of the Three Young Men) is a deuterocanonical addition to Daniel, preserved in the RSV Latin version. It is a liturgical canticle used in Christian worship and of interest to scholars of biblical apocrypha and Septuagint studies.

title: Proverbs
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Proverbs in Latin, a foundational wisdom text.

title: Psalms
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Psalms are a collection of religious hymns and prayers, central to both Jewish and Christian liturgy. They are among the most widely read and influential texts in the Western tradition, noted for their poetic beauty and spiritual depth.

title: Qohelet (Ecclesiastes)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Latin text of Ecclesiastes from the RSV tradition. Its presence in a Latin corpus is likely a cataloging artifact; the work itself is a canonical wisdom book.

title: Revelation (Apocalypse)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Revelation (Apocalypse) is the final book of the New Testament, attributed to John of Patmos. It contains apocalyptic visions and prophecies, and has been highly influential in Christian eschatology and art.

title: Romans
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: Paul's Epistle to the Romans is a foundational theological text in Christianity, expounding on justification, grace, and salvation. It is one of the most studied and influential books of the New Testament.

title: Ruth
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Ruth is a short narrative in the Old Testament, found here in the RSV. It tells the story of Ruth's loyalty to Naomi and her eventual marriage to Boaz. It is a canonical religious text, valued for its literary beauty, theological themes, and importance in biblical studies.

title: Sirach (Ben Sira, Ecclesiasticus)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Sirach (Ecclesiasticus) in a Latin version, a deuterocanonical wisdom book. It has been valued for moral instruction and its influence on liturgy and ethics.

title: Susanna
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The story of Susanna, a deuterocanonical addition to Daniel, is found in the Latin Vulgate. It is a popular narrative of virtue and divine justice, often studied in biblical and artistic contexts.

title: Titus
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: This Latin version of the Epistle to Titus, drawn from the Revised Standard Version tradition, is a modern rendering. Its inclusion in the corpus attests to the ongoing reception of biblical texts in Latin.

title: Tobit
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Tobit in the RSV, a deuterocanonical work of religious fiction about Tobit and his son Tobias. It is valued for its piety and moral lessons.

title: Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia) from the RSV is a deuterocanonical wisdom book. It is widely read in Catholic and Orthodox traditions and studied for its Hellenistic Jewish theology.

title: Zechariah (Zachariah)
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Zechariah from the Revised Standard Version of the English Bible is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. While the RSV is a modern translation, the text is a major religious and literary work.

title: Zephaniah
author: English Bible (RSV)
classification_notes: The Book of Zephaniah in Latin, a minor prophetic book.

title: carmina, fragmentum
author: Fabius Dossennus
classification_notes: A single 5-word fragment from the 'carmina' of Fabius Dossennus, an early Latin dramatist. The work's extreme brevity and uncertain context renders it a minimal trace of Roman comic or satiric drama.

title: Annales

classification_notes: Fragments of Fabius Pictor's Annales, one of the earliest Roman historical works. Written in Latin, it covers early Roman history and is cited by later authors like Livy.

title: Iuris Pontificis Libri
author: Fabius Pictor
classification_notes: Fabius Pictor's Iuris Pontificis Libri are fragments of a work on pontifical law, a key source for early Roman religious and legal institutions. The surviving 106 words are meager but valuable for specialists.

title: oratio
author: Favorinus
classification_notes: Favorinus' oratio survives only in a fragment. As a work of a noted Greek rhetor and philosopher active in Rome, it is of specialist interest for the study of the Second Sophistic and Latin-Greek bilingualism.

title: Annales
author: Fenestella
classification_notes: Fenestella's Annales survive only in a few fragments. He was a Roman historian of the early imperial period whose work covered Roman antiquities; the fragments are valuable for studies of lost historiography.

title: De Orthographia
author: Flavius Caper
classification_notes: Flavius Caper's 'De Orthographia' is a late antique grammatical treatise on Latin spelling and orthography. Its moderate length (2409 words) and technical focus make it a specialist resource for historical linguistics and textual criticism.

title: De Verbis Dubiis
author: Flavius Caper
classification_notes: Flavius Caper's De Verbis Dubiis, a grammatical treatise on doubtful Latin words and usage. It is an important source for understanding late Latin linguistic debates.

title: De Accentibus
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: The 'De Accentibus' from the Fragmenta Bobiensia is a late antique grammatical treatise on Latin accents. It is a specialist work, of interest to scholars of Latin grammar and late antique education.

title: De Finalibus Syllabis
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: De Finalibus Syllabis is a Late Antique grammatical treatise preserved in fragmentary form among the Bobbio manuscripts. The work focuses on the metrical and phonetic endings of Latin syllables, contributing to the tradition of Latin grammatical scholarship. Its preservation as fragments limits its circulation, but it remains of specialist interest for the history of Latin linguistics.

title: De Littera
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: The Fragmenta Bobiensia are a collection of grammatical excerpts from a late antique manuscript, here the portion De Littera. These fragments preserve otherwise lost grammatical discussions and are of interest primarily to specialists in Latin philology and the history of grammar. The authorship is unknown and the text is transmitted only in a single witness.

title: De Metris
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: De Metris from the Fragmenta Bobiensia is a brief grammatical treatise on Latin meter, surviving in a fragmentary state. It is an obscure specialist text, of interest primarily to historians of Latin grammar and metric theory.

title: De Nomine
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: De Nomine, from the Fragmenta Bobiensia, is a Late Antique grammatical treatise on the noun. It is a specialist source for the study of Latin grammar and pedagogy in the post-Roman world.

title: De Propriis Nominibus
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: The Fragmenta Bobiensia are a set of Latin grammatical fragments, possibly scholia or notes on proper nouns (De Propriis Nominibus). With only 97 words, the text is extremely brief and survives only in excerpted form. Its scholarly value lies in the evidence it provides for late antique grammatical teaching, but it has negligible readership beyond specialists in Latin grammatical fragments.

title: De Structuris
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: The Fragmenta Bobiensia, including De Structuris, are grammatical fragments from Bobbio. They are specialist texts for the study of late antique Latin grammar and textual transmission.

title: De Versibus
author: Fragmenta Bobiensia
classification_notes: Fragmenta Bobiensia on Latin verse forms.

title: Gai Institutionum epitome
author: Gaius
classification_notes: The 'Gai Institutionum epitome' is a summary of Gaius's 'Institutes,' a foundational textbook of Roman law. It is a commonly cited source in legal history, essential for understanding the structure of Roman legal education.

title: Institut., frr. Aeg. et Oxyrh.
author: Gaius
classification_notes: Gaius' Institutiones, fragmenta Aegyptiaca et Oxyrhynchia are important fragments of his foundational textbook on Roman law. Despite their fragmentary state, they are a key source for the study of Roman legal education and the Institutes' structure.

title: Institutiones
author: Gaius
classification_notes: Gaius' Institutiones, the foundational textbook of Roman law. It provides a systematic exposition of private law and is the principal source for early Roman legal education.

title: Argonautica

title: tragoediae

title: iurisprudentia

title: Cynegetica

title: 2 Thessalonians
