author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Jonah, part of the Hebrew Bible, is a short prophetic narrative that gained wide circulation in Latin through the Vulgate. Its account of the prophet's mission to Nineveh and the episode with the great fish makes it a staple of biblical literature, studied in religious and literary contexts.

title: Joshua
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: Joshua in the Latin tradition (likely reflecting the Vulgate or Vetus Latina) is a major historical book of the Old Testament, recounting the conquest of Canaan. It is foundational for biblical history and theology.

title: Judges
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Judges from the Hebrew Bible in Latin translation recounts the history of Israel before the monarchy. It is a canonical historical book, widely studied for its narratives and themes of leadership and covenant.

title: Lamentations
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: Lamentations from the Hebrew Bible (MT) is a poetic book mourning the destruction of Jerusalem. It is central to Jewish liturgy and widely read in biblical studies.

title: Leviticus
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Latin version of Leviticus, the third book of the Pentateuch, containing priestly laws. It is a key text in the Latin biblical tradition.

title: Malachi
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: Malachi is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible, the last book of the Minor Prophets. This Latin version derives from the Masoretic Text tradition. It deals with covenant faithfulness and the coming messenger.

title: Micah
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Micah from the Hebrew Bible (Masoretic Text) is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. It is a significant text for biblical studies and the history of prophecy.

title: Nahum
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Nahum, from the Hebrew Bible presented in Latin, is a brief prophetic oracle against Nineveh, preserved in the Latin biblical tradition.

title: Nehemiah
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Nehemiah from the Hebrew Bible, recounting Nehemiah's efforts to rebuild Jerusalem's walls and reform the community. The Latin version is part of the Vulgate tradition.

title: Numbers
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Numbers from the Hebrew Bible is a central religious text, fundamental to Jewish and Christian traditions. Its Latin versions, such as the Vulgate, have been widely copied and studied throughout the medieval and early modern periods, making it a major work in Latin religious literature.

title: Proverbs
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Proverbs, a collection of wisdom sayings, is a central text of the Hebrew Bible. Its Latin version is widely used in Christian liturgy and moral instruction.

title: Psalms
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Psalms in the Masoretic Text (BHS) is the central collection of Hebrew hymns, prayers, and liturgical poetry. It is a cornerstone of the Hebrew Bible and has profoundly influenced both Jewish and Christian worship and literature.

title: Qohelet (Ecclesiastes)
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: Qohelet (Ecclesiastes) from the Hebrew Bible (MT) is a wisdom book reflecting on the meaning of life. Its philosophical and existential themes have made it widely read and studied.

title: Ruth
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Ruth from the Latin Vulgate is a short biblical narrative of loyalty and redemption. As part of the canonical Old Testament, it is widely read in religious and literary contexts, though its Latin version is of particular interest to Vulgate scholars.

title: Zechariah (Zachariah)
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Zechariah from the Hebrew Bible, a major prophetic book.

title: Zephaniah
author: Hebrew Bible (MT or BHS)
classification_notes: The Book of Zephaniah, a minor prophetic book in the Hebrew Bible, is a brief oracle of judgment and restoration. In its Latin Vulgate form, it contributes to the corpus of Old Testament prophecy, studied for its theological themes and historical context in the late monarchic period of Judah.

title: oratio
author: Helvius Mancia
classification_notes: This fragmentary 'oratio' by Helvius Mancia is a very brief remnant of a speech. It is an obscure text, known only from indirect transmission. Its interest is limited to specialists in fragmentary Roman oratory.

title: carmina
author: Hilarius Arelatensis
classification_notes: A fragment of carmina by Hilarius Arelatensis (Hilary of Arles), a fifth-century bishop. These poetic remnants are of minor literary significance, mainly of interest for late antique Gallic Christianity.

title: Ilias Latina
author: Homerus Latinus
classification_notes: The Ilias Latina is a Latin epitome of Homer's Iliad, attributed to Baebius Italicus. It was used as a school text in antiquity and is studied for its reception of Homer and Latin poetic technique.

title: Ars Poetica
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's 'Ars Poetica' is a verse epistle on the art of poetry, enormously influential on Western literary theory. It sets out principles of drama, style, and imitation.

title: Carmen Saeculare
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's Carmen Saeculare was commissioned by Augustus for the Secular Games of 17 BCE. It is a hymn performed by a chorus of youths, praising the gods and the Augustan regime. One of Horace's most famous occasional poems.

title: Carmina
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's Carmina (Odes) is a collection of lyric poems across four books, considered one of the supreme achievements of Latin poetry. The Odes explore themes of love, friendship, mortality, and politics with exquisite artistry. They are a canonical text in Latin literature, central to the study of ancient poetry and metrics.

title: Epistulae
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's Epistulae are verse letters treating philosophical and literary themes with urbane wit and moral insight. They are a centerpiece of Augustan poetry and a key source for Roman literary culture and ethical thought.

title: Epodi
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's 'Epodi' (Epodes) are a collection of iambic poems, showcasing his mastery of meter and satirical voice. This work is a major component of the Augustan poetic corpus, widely read and studied for its literary innovation and historical context.

title: Sermones
author: Horace
classification_notes: Horace's Sermones (Satires) are foundational works of Roman satire, blending moral philosophy with urbane wit. They are widely read and studied for their literary innovation and social commentary.

title: Bellum Histricum
author: Hostius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Hostius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Hostius's Bellum Histricum, a fragmentary epic on the Istrian War, is one of the earliest historical epics of Latin literature, preserving almost nothing beyond a few lines.

title: Fabulae
author: Hyginus
classification_notes: Hyginus' Fabulae is a collection of mythological narratives and genealogies, likely compiled in the 2nd century AD. It is a key source for Greek and Roman mythography, frequently consulted by classicists and students of mythology. The work is neither highly literary nor original but serves as a reference manual for myths.

title: Astronomica
author: Hyginus Astronomus
classification_notes: Hyginus's Astronomica is a handbook on mythology and astronomy, often used for reference. It is a key source for ancient constellations and their associated myths, though its scientific value is limited.

title: Constitutio Limitum sp.
author: Hyginus Gromaticus
classification_notes: Hyginus Gromaticus' Constitutio Limitum is a technical treatise on land surveying and the establishment of boundaries. It belongs to the corpus of Roman agrimensores (land surveyors). It is a specialist text for the history of surveying, Roman land law, and practical geometry.

title: De Condicionibus Agrorum
author: Hyginus Gromaticus
classification_notes: Hyginus Gromaticus's De Condicionibus Agrorum is a technical manual on land surveying and classification. It is a key text for understanding Roman land management and the work of the agrimensores.

title: De Generibus Controversiarum
author: Hyginus Gromaticus
classification_notes: Hyginus Gromaticus' De Generibus Controversiarum is a technical manual on land surveying and boundary disputes. It is an essential source for Roman land management and legal geography.

title: De Limitibus
author: Hyginus Gromaticus
classification_notes: Hyginus Gromaticus' De Limitibus, a manual on Roman surveying and the establishment of boundary lines. It is a key text for understanding Roman land division (centuriation).

title: De Munition. Castrorum sp.
author: Hyginus Gromaticus
classification_notes: Hyginus Gromaticus's 'De Munitionibus Castrorum' (On the Fortification of Camps) is a Roman technical manual on military engineering. It is a specialist text for those studying Roman military practices and surveying.

title: Dacica
author: Imperator M. Ulpius Traianus
classification_notes: Trajan's 'Dacica' is a lost historical work on the Dacian Wars, known only from a single word. It is an obscure text, of interest only to specialists in Roman imperial history and lost literature.

title: carmina
author: Imperator Nero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Nero Emperor of Rome 37-68; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: These fragmentary verses attributed to the emperor Nero reflect his poetic ambitions, though their quality and authenticity have been debated. They survive only in quotations.

title: oratio
author: Iulius Africanus
classification_notes: Only 11 words survive of an oratio (speech) by Iulius Africanus, a 1st-century AD rhetorician. The fragment is too short to recover any rhetorical context. It is of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Latin oratory.

title: historiae
author: Iulius Atherianus
classification_notes: A fragment of 'historiae' by Iulius Atherianus, a little-known historical writer; only a few words survive.

title: grammatica
author: Iulius Modestus
classification_notes: Fragments of grammatical writings by Iulius Modestus, a Latin grammarian of the Augustan era. These fragments preserve linguistic observations from the early Roman grammatical tradition.

title: carmina
author: Iulius Montanus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Julius Montanus d. 27; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Iulius Montanus' carmina are preserved in only 37 words, likely fragments of epic or elegiac poetry. He is a minor figure known primarily from references in other authors, and his work is of interest only to the most specialized philologists.

title: carmina
author: Iulius Valerius
classification_notes: These carmina by Iulius Valerius are fragmentary poems, likely from late antiquity. They are of obscure interest, known only from sparse manuscript evidence.

title: Digesta Iustiniani
author: Iustinianus
classification_notes: The 'Digesta Iustiniani' is a massive compilation of Roman jurisprudence, commissioned by Emperor Justinian and a cornerstone of civil law. Its influence on Western legal tradition is immense, studied continuously from the medieval period to modern legal scholarship.

title: palliatae
author: Iuventius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Juventius 2nd cent. B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Iuventius' palliatae fragments, Roman comedy in Greek dress.

title: Defensionem Regiam (Latin Works, vol. 7, pp. 1-300)
author: John Milton (English and Latin)
classification_notes: John Milton's Latin prose work Defensionem Regiam, a defense of the English Commonwealth, is part of his Latin writings. It is significant for Milton's political thought and Renaissance Latin prose style.

title: Paradise Lost (English)
author: John Milton (English and Latin)
classification_notes: Paradise Lost is Milton's epic masterpiece, written in English but with strong Latin influences. It is a canonical work of world literature, despite the language tag indicating Latin for the row.

title: Anticato
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Caesar's Anticato, a lost polemical work against Cato the Younger, known only from fragments.

title: Bellum Civile
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Caesar's 'Bellum Civile' is a first-hand account of the Roman civil war, a foundational text for Latin historiography and military history. It is a canonical work, central to the study of Latin literature and Roman history.

title: De Analogia
author: Julius Caesar
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Caesar, Julius; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Caesar's De Analogia survives only in fragments, but it represents his contribution to Latin grammatical theory, arguing for analogy over anomaly. The work is important for the history of linguistics, though its fragmentary state limits its impact.

title: De Bello Gallico
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Caesar's De Bello Gallico, a firsthand account of the Gallic Wars. It is a foundational work of Latin historiography, admired for its clarity and narrative power.

title: carmina
author: Julius Caesar
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Caesar, Julius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: These fragmentary 'carmina' attributed to Julius Caesar are likely poetic works, possibly epic in nature. The attribution is uncertain, and the fragments are too brief for firm classification. They are of specialist interest for the study of Caesar's literary output.

title: epistulae ad Ciceronem
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Fragments of Caesar's letters to Cicero, preserving their personal correspondence. These fragments offer insight into the political relationship between Caesar and Cicero.

title: epistulae ad familiares
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Caesar's epistulae ad familiares are fragmentary letters to his family, surviving only in excerpts. They are of specialist interest to scholars of Caesar's correspondence and late Republican epistolography.

title: orationes
author: Julius Caesar
classification_notes: Caesar's orationes survive only in fragments. As a renowned orator and statesman, his speeches are of interest to historians of Roman rhetoric and politics, but the extant text is very brief.

title: Saturae
author: Juvenal
classification_notes: Juvenal's Saturae are the pinnacle of Roman verse satire, written in the early Imperial period. These sixteen poems excoriate Roman society with bitter wit and rhetorical force, establishing the canonical model for the genre.

title: carmina
author: L. Accius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Accius, Lucius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Accius' carmina are fragments of his epic and tragic poetry, surviving only in quotations. As a major early Latin poet, his fragments are of specialist interest to scholars of Roman literature and textual transmission.

title: praetextae
author: L. Accius
classification_notes: The praetextae (historical plays) of L. Accius, a major figure in Roman Republican tragedy. Only fragments survive, totaling 292 words. These fragments are our main evidence for the Roman historical drama (fabula praetexta), a genre that dramatized events from Roman history. Accius' praetextae are of specialist interest to scholars of Roman theater, Republican literature, and the performance of Roman identity.

title: tragoediae
author: L. Accius
classification_notes: Accius' tragoediae survive only in fragments. As a major Roman tragic poet, his work is of specialist interest for the study of Republican drama and its reception of Greek tragedy.

title: grammatica
author: L. Aelius Praeconinus Stilo
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Stilo Praeconinus, L. Aelius (Lucius Aelius) approximately 154 B.C.-approximately 74 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Grammatical fragments of L. Aelius Stilo, an early Roman scholar and teacher of Varro. His work on Latin grammar was influential but survives only in quotations.

title: oratio
author: L. Aemilius L. f. M. n. Paulus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Paullus, Lucius Aemilius approximately 229 B.C.-160 B.C; perseus_editor=Meyer, Heinrich; perseus_year_published=1842; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A fragment of a speech by L. Aemilius Paullus, a Roman general and orator; known only from brief citations.

title: togatae
author: L. Afranius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Afranius, Lucius; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The togatae (Roman comedy in Italian settings) of L. Afranius survive in substantial fragments. These plays adapt Menandrian themes to Roman life, providing important evidence for the development of Roman comedy after Plautus and Terence. They are commonly studied by specialists in Republican drama.

title: Liber Memorialis
author: L. Ampelius
classification_notes: Ampelius' Liber Memorialis, a concise handbook of world history and geography.

title: grammatica
author: L. Annaeus Cornutus
classification_notes: The grammatical work of L. Annaeus Cornutus, a 1st-century CE Stoic philosopher and teacher, survives only in fragments. This text, labeled 'grammatica', likely addressed linguistic and literary topics. Though brief, it is valuable for the history of Latin grammatical thought.

title: Historiae Belli Punici
author: L. Arruntius
classification_notes: The history of the Punic Wars by L. Arruntius survives only in a tiny fragment. Arruntius was a Roman historian of the late Republic or early Empire.

title: grammatica
author: L. Ateius Praetextatus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Ateius Praetextatus, L. (Lucius) fl. 86-29 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A fragment of grammatical writing by L. Ateius Praetextatus, a late Republican scholar. The brevity of the text limits its utility.

title: carmina
author: L. Aurelius Avianius Symmachus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Symmachus, L. Aurelius Avianus d. ca. 376; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Symmachus's poems are fragmentary, reflecting the literary activity of a late antique senator and orator.

title: Annales
author: L. Calpurnius Piso Censorinus Frugi
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Piso Frugi, Lucius Calpurnius b. ca. 182 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Piso's Annales are fragments of a historical work covering early Roman history. They are among the earliest Roman historical writings and are cited by later authors, but the fragments are too small to reconstruct the original.

title: oratio
author: L. Cassius Longinus
classification_notes: The speech (oratio) of L. Cassius Longinus, a Roman jurist and politician, is known only from a few words. The fragment is too small for rhetorical analysis but is occasionally cited in studies of late Republican legal oratory.

title: grammatica
author: L. Cincius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Cincius, L. (Lucius); perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The grammatical fragments of L. Cincius, preserved in Funaioli's collection, represent early Roman grammatical scholarship. Though only 118 words survive, Cincius's work on Latin language structure is of specialist interest for the history of Latin linguistics and the development of grammatical theory at Rome.

title: iurisprudentia
author: L. Cincius
classification_notes: L. Cincius was a Roman jurist of the late Republic. His work on jurisprudence ('iurisprudentia') survives only in fragments, but it contributed to the development of Roman legal thought. This text is a key source for early Roman law.

title: grammatica
author: L. Cincius Alimentus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Cincius Alimentus, L. (Lucius) fl. 210 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cincius Alimentus' grammatica survives only in a few fragments. As an early Roman grammatical work, it is of interest to historians of Latin linguistics, but its brevity limits its readership.

title: Annales
author: L. Coelius Antipater
classification_notes: The Annales of L. Coelius Antipater, a pioneering Roman historian. Surviving in 413 words of fragments, this work was a major source for the Second Punic War and was praised for its stylistic innovation. The fragments are of specialist interest to historians of Roman historiography and the development of Latin historical writing.

title: Historiae
author: L. Cornelius Sisenna
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Sisenna, L. Cornelius (Lucius Cornelius) ca. 118-67 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of Sisenna's Historiae, a historical work covering the Social and Mithridatic Wars. Sisenna was a contemporary of Sulla and an important early Roman historian.

title: Milesiae
author: L. Cornelius Sisenna
classification_notes: A fragment of Cornelius Sisenna's Milesiae, an early Latin erotic narrative in the style of Aristides' Milesian Tales. Only 69 words survive, but it is a rare witness to the genre.

title: Commentarii Rerum Gestarum
author: L. Cornelius Sulla
classification_notes: Sulla's 'Commentarii Rerum Gestarum' were his autobiography/memoirs, now lost except for fragments. They were a key source for later historians.

title: oratio
author: L. Herennius Balbus
classification_notes: A fragment of a speech by L. Herennius Balbus, a minor orator of the Late Republic; survives in a single citation.

title: Auspiciorum Liber, fragmenta
author: L. Iulius Caesar
classification_notes: A fragmentary work on auspices (Auspiciorum Liber) attributed to L. Iulius Caesar, a late Republican antiquarian. Only brief quotations survive, providing limited insight into Roman augural lore.

title: orationes
author: L. Licinius Crassus
classification_notes: L. Licinius Crassus's orations, known from fragments, represent an important witness to late Republican eloquence and political debate.

title: Odyssia
author: L. Livius Andronicus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Andronicus, Livius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Livius Andronicus' Odyssia is a pioneering work of Latin literature, a translation/adaptation of Homer's Odyssey into Saturnian verse. Only 320 words survive in fragments, but it marks the beginning of Latin epic and literary translation. It is studied primarily by specialists in early Latin literature and the history of Roman literary culture.

title: palliatae
author: L. Livius Andronicus
classification_notes: Livius Andronicus' palliatae, fragments of early Roman comedy.

title: tragoediae
author: L. Livius Andronicus
classification_notes: The tragedies of L. Livius Andronicus, the earliest known Roman poet, survive in fragments that show his pioneering translations of Greek drama. These are essential for understanding the origins of Latin literature and the adaptation of Greek genres in Rome, commonly studied by specialists in archaic Latin.

title: orationes
author: L. Marcius Philippus
classification_notes: The orations of L. Marcius Philippus, a Roman politician and orator of the Late Republic, survive only in negligible fragments. Their existence is noted primarily for prosopographical studies of the period, as the content is too meager for rhetorical analysis.

title: fr. in fragmentis Vaticanis
author: L. Neratius Priscus
classification_notes: A tiny fragment from the jurist Neratius Priscus preserved in the Fragmenta Vaticana. It provides a glimpse into Roman legal reasoning but is too brief to contribute substantially beyond its source context.

title: oratio
author: L. Novius
classification_notes: A 35-word fragment of a speech by L. Novius, an orator of the late Republic. Very little is known about Novius or this work.

title: grammatica
author: L. Orbilius Pupillus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Orbilius Pupillus, L. (Lucius) approximately 114 B.C.-approximately 14 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: This grammatical work by Orbilius, a noted grammarian of the late Republic, survives only in fragments. It is of specialist interest for the history of Latin grammatical scholarship.

title: Atellanae
author: L. Pomponius Bononiensis
classification_notes: The Atellanae of L. Pomponius Bononiensis are fragments of the Atellan farce, a popular form of comic drama at Rome. These plays, featuring stock characters, are important for understanding Roman popular theater and the development of comic genres. The surviving fragments (1423 words) are studied by specialists in Roman drama and the history of comedy.

title: orationes
author: L. Quinctius
classification_notes: Fragments of speeches by L. Quinctius, a minor orator of the late Republic.

title: carmina
author: L. Varius Rufus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Varius Rufus, Lucius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of poems by Varius Rufus, an Augustan poet known for epic and tragedy, preserved indirectly. The scant remains provide limited evidence of his work, but he is an important figure for the history of Augustan literature.

title: tragoediae
author: L. Varius Rufus
classification_notes: The tragedies of L. Varius Rufus, a poet of the Augustan circle, survive only in fragments, including his celebrated 'Thyestes' praised by Quintilian. Despite the loss, these remains are highly valued by specialists for their influence on later drama and their place in Augustan literary culture.

title: epigramma
author: L. Verginius Rufus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Verginius Rufus, L. (Lucius) 14-97; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: This single epigram by L. Verginius Rufus is a very brief fragment. It is an obscure piece, known only from a collection of fragments. Its interest is limited to specialists in early imperial Latin poetry.

title: Assis Distributio ...
author: L. Volusius Maecianus
classification_notes: Volusius Maecianus's Assis Distributio, a Roman mathematical treatise on the division of the as (a unit of currency or weight). This work illustrates applied mathematics in the Roman world.

title: iurisprudentia, fragmenta
author: Laelius Felix
classification_notes: Laelius Felix was a Roman jurist of the 2nd century AD. His iurisprudentia survives only in 140 words of fragments cited in later legal compilations. The work pertains to Roman civil law. It is highly obscure, consulted only by specialists in Roman jurisprudence and legal history.

title: carmina
author: Laevius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Laevius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Laevius' carmina survive in a few fragments (302 words) of experimental lyric poetry. He is known for his innovative meters and style, but his work is too fragmentary for easy classification; it is primarily of specialist interest.

title: fr. dubium a Morel omissum
author: Laevius
classification_notes: A disputed and minuscule fragment of the poet Laevius, omitted by Morel. Its authenticity is uncertain, and it contributes marginally to the reconstruction of his poetic output.

title: 1 Chronicles
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 1 Chronicles in the Vulgate is a historical book of the Old Testament, recapitulating the narrative from Adam to David. It is important for the study of biblical historiography and temple traditions.

title: 1 Corinthians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The First Epistle to the Corinthians in the Vulgate translation, a central Pauline text of the Christian canon.

title: 1 Esdras (3 Esdras)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 1 Esdras (also known as 3 Esdras in some traditions) is a deuterocanonical book found in the Latin Vulgate appendix. It retells the history of the return from the Babylonian exile, parallel to the canonical Ezra-Nehemiah. It is of significant interest to scholars of the Apocrypha and the history of the biblical canon.

title: 1 John
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The First Epistle of John from the Latin Vulgate Bible. This New Testament letter, traditionally attributed to John the Evangelist, addresses themes of love, light, and Christian fellowship. The Vulgate version, translated by Jerome, was the authoritative Latin text for over a millennium. It is a major religious and literary text widely read in theological and historical contexts.

title: 1 Kings (3 Reigns)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The first book of Kings (3 Reigns in the Vulgate) recounts the rise and fall of the Israelite monarchy. This Latin version, part of the Vulgate Bible, has been central to Western liturgy and scholarship.

title: 1 Maccabees
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The First Book of Maccabees in Latin, a historical narrative of the Jewish revolt.

title: 1 Peter
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 1 Peter, a New Testament epistle, here from the Latin Vulgate, addresses Christian suffering and hope. It is a key text for early Christian theology and ecclesiology.

title: 1 Samuel (1 Reigns)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 1 Samuel (1 Reigns) from the Vulgate details the rise of the monarchy in Israel, focusing on Samuel, Saul, and David.

title: 1 Thessalonians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The First Epistle to the Thessalonians in the Latin Vulgate, one of Paul's earliest letters. This Latin version has been central to Christian theology and liturgy.

title: 1 Timothy
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The First Epistle to Timothy from the Latin Vulgate is a Pauline pastoral letter. This Latin translation is a major work within the Christian biblical tradition, widely used in the Western Church.

title: 2 Chronicles
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Second Book of Chronicles from the Latin Vulgate is a historical book of the Old Testament. This Latin translation is a major work within the Christian biblical tradition.

title: 2 Corinthians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 2 Corinthians from the Latin Vulgate is a major Pauline epistle, widely read for its theological depth and personal apostolic narrative. It is a canonical New Testament text, central to Christian doctrine and biblical studies.

title: 2 Kings (4 Reigns)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 2 Kings (4 Reigns) from the Vulgate continues the history of the Israelite monarchy, covering the period from Elijah to the Babylonian exile.

title: 2 Maccabees
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Second Book of Maccabees from the Latin Vulgate, a deuterocanonical historical work detailing the Jewish revolt against Seleucid rule. Its Latin version is widely used in Christian tradition.

title: 2 Peter
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 2 Peter is a canonical New Testament epistle.

title: 2 Samuel (2 Reigns)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 2 Samuel (2 Reigns) is a historical book in the Vulgate. It is of specialist interest within biblical history.

title: 2 Timothy
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Second Epistle to Timothy in the Latin Vulgate, one of Paul's pastoral epistles. This Latin version has been influential in Christian doctrine and church order.

title: 3 John
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 3 John is a brief New Testament epistle traditionally attributed to John the Evangelist. It addresses early Christian hospitality and authority, and is part of the Johannine corpus. Its limited length and specialized content make it less widely read than the Gospels.

title: 4 Ezra (4 Esdras) (2 Esdras)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: 4 Ezra (2 Esdras) in the Vulgate, an apocalyptic apocryphal book.

title: Acts
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Acts of the Apostles from the Latin Vulgate is the canonical New Testament narrative of the early Christian church. It is a central text for biblical studies, early Christian history, and Latin patristic literature. Its length (nearly 17,000 words) and narrative scope contribute to its major status.

title: Amos
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Amos is a prophetic book from the Vulgate.

title: Baruch
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Baruch, attributed to the scribe of Jeremiah, is a deuterocanonical work in the Latin Vulgate tradition. It contains prayers, wisdom, and an exhortation to repentance. While less prominent in the canon, it remains important for patristic exegesis and liturgical use.

title: Canticles (Song of Solomon, Song of Songs)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Song of Solomon (Canticles), from the Latin Vulgate, is a poetic book of the Bible. Its allegorical interpretation has been central in Christian mysticism and theology.

title: Colossians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Colossians from the Latin Vulgate is a Pauline letter emphasizing the supremacy of Christ and correct Christian living. It is a core New Testament text, central to Christian doctrine and frequently cited in Latin theological and exegetical works.

title: Daniel
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Daniel from the Latin Vulgate Bible, translated by Jerome in the late 4th to early 5th century. This prophetic and apocalyptic book was highly influential in both Jewish and Christian traditions, and the Vulgate version served as the standard biblical text in Western Christianity for over a millennium. The Latin text of Daniel, including the additions found in the Septuagint and Vulgate traditions, is a major religious and literary text with wide readership.

title: Deuteronomy
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Deuteronomy from the Latin Vulgate is a foundational book of the Old Testament, containing laws and narratives. It is widely read in religious and scholarly contexts, and its Latin version is central to Vulgate studies.

title: Ephesians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Ephesians from the Vulgate.

title: Esther
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Esther from the Latin Vulgate is a narrative of Jewish deliverance in the Persian court. It is commonly read in religious and literary contexts, and its Latin version is important for Vulgate textual criticism.

title: Exodus
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Exodus from the Latin Vulgate is a foundational book of the Old Testament, recounting the liberation of the Israelites from Egypt. It is widely read in religious and scholarly contexts, and its Latin version is central to Vulgate studies.

title: Ezekiel
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Ezekiel is a major prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible, containing visions of the glory of God and the restoration of Israel. This Vulgate version was translated by Jerome and is foundational for Christian theology.

title: Ezra (1 Esdras)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Ezra (1 Esdras) from the Latin Vulgate is an important historical book of the Old Testament. Jerome's translation has been a standard text in the Latin Church, widely studied and used in liturgy.

title: Galatians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Galatians from the Latin Vulgate is a Pauline letter defending justification by faith and apostolic authority. It is a cornerstone of Christian theology, widely cited in patristic and Reformation debates.

title: Genesis
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Genesis from the Latin Vulgate, the foundational book of the Bible.

title: Habakkuk
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Habakkuk from the Latin Vulgate is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. The Vulgate is the standard Latin Bible of the Western Church, central to medieval and early modern Christianity.

title: Hebrews
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Hebrews from the Latin Vulgate is a New Testament letter of uncertain authorship, long attributed to Paul but now considered anonymous. It is theologically significant for its discussion of Christ as high priest. It is widely read in biblical scholarship and Latin patristic tradition.

title: Hosea
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Hosea in the Vulgate is a prophetic book of the Old Testament, known for its powerful marriage metaphor for God's relationship with Israel. It is a staple of biblical studies and theological interpretation.

title: Isaiah
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Isaiah from the Latin Vulgate is a major prophetic text of the Old Testament, rich in messianic prophecy and liturgical use.

title: James
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle of James from the Latin Vulgate is a canonical New Testament book. Its ethical teachings and emphasis on faith and works have made it a widely read and theologically significant text.

title: Jeremiah
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Jerome's Vulgate translation of Jeremiah, a major prophetic book. The Vulgate became the standard Latin Bible in the West.

title: Job
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Job in the Vulgate is a profound poetic and philosophical book grappling with theodicy. Its Latin version is central to Western theological discourse and has shaped literary and ethical thought.

title: Joel
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Vulgate version of Joel, translated by Jerome, is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. It is part of the canonical Latin Bible and has been fundamental to Western Christianity.

title: John
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Gospel of John in the Latin Vulgate, a foundational Christian text central to theology and liturgy. Its Latin translation by Jerome has been immensely influential.

title: Jonah
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Jonah in the Vulgate is a short prophetic narrative of singular literary and theological importance. Its themes of repentance and divine mercy have resonated across Judeo-Christian tradition.

title: Joshua
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Joshua from the Latin Vulgate is a foundational historical and religious text. Jerome's translation has been a standard in Western Christianity for centuries, ensuring its major status.

title: Judges
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Judges from the Latin Vulgate Bible. This historical book recounts the period of the judges in ancient Israel, a cycle of apostasy, oppression, and deliverance. The Vulgate version of Judges, translated by Jerome, is a core text of the Latin biblical tradition. It is a major text for students and scholars of the Bible, Latin literature, and religious history.

title: Judith
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Judith from the Latin Vulgate, a deuterocanonical book of the Old Testament.

title: Lamentations
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Lamentations from the Latin Vulgate is a poetic mourning for Jerusalem's fall, attributed to Jeremiah, central to Christian liturgy.

title: Leviticus
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Leviticus, part of the Latin Vulgate, is the third book of the Torah and the Christian Old Testament. It details the priestly code and laws of ritual purity. As a segment of the canonical Vulgate Bible, it holds central importance in Judeo-Christian religious tradition and Latin biblical studies.

title: Luke
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Gospel of Luke in the Vulgate is one of the four canonical Gospels, essential to Christian theology and narrative. Its detailed account of Jesus' life and parables is foundational for Western culture.

title: Malachi
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Malachi is a minor prophetic book in the Vulgate. It has a place in biblical studies but is less prominent than major prophets, making it of specialist interest.

title: Mark
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Gospel of Mark from the Latin Vulgate is the second synoptic gospel, known for its vivid narrative and focus on Jesus' ministry. It is a canonical New Testament text, central to Christian liturgy and scholarly study of the historical Jesus.

title: Matthew
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Gospel of Matthew from the Latin Vulgate is the first synoptic gospel, central to Christian teaching and liturgy. It is a canonical text, immensely influential in Western theology, art, and culture.

title: Micah
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Micah from the Latin Vulgate is a prophetic book of the Old Testament, known for its messages of justice and hope. It is commonly read in religious and scholarly contexts.

title: Nahum
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Nahum from the Latin Vulgate is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible. Its vivid poetry and themes of divine judgment are studied in biblical and literary contexts.

title: Nehemiah (2 Esdras)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Nehemiah in the Vulgate, a historical book of the Old Testament.

title: Numbers
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Vulgate version of Numbers, translated primarily by Jerome, recounts the Israelites' wilderness wanderings, census, and legal codes. As a core Pentateuchal book in the Latin Bible, it is essential for the study of Christian scripture and Latin biblical tradition.

title: Philippians
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Philippians in the Vulgate, a Pauline letter.

title: Prayer of Manasseh
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Prayer of Manasseh from the Latin Vulgate is a brief deuterocanonical penitential prayer. It is a specialist text, of interest primarily to biblical scholars and students of the Apocrypha.

title: Proverbs
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Proverbs from the Latin Vulgate is a wisdom book of the Hebrew Bible. Its aphoristic style and ethical teachings have made it widely read in religious and literary contexts.

title: Psalm 151
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Psalm 151 is a short deuterocanonical psalm in the Vulgate. It is of specialist interest within biblical studies, often included in scholarly editions of the Psalms.

title: Psalms (from the Hebrew)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Psalms from the Hebrew in the Vulgate. The Psalter has been central to Christian worship and Latin literature, with profound influence on liturgy, music, and spirituality.

title: Psalms (from the Old Greek)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Vulgate Psalms, translated from the Old Greek (Septuagint), are a foundational collection of hymns and prayers in the Latin Christian tradition. They have been central to liturgy, theology, and devotional life for over a millennium and are among the most widely circulated and studied biblical texts.

title: Qohelet (Ecclesiastes)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The book of Qohelet (Ecclesiastes) from the Latin Vulgate is a wisdom book of the Hebrew Bible, translated by Jerome. It is known for its skeptical and philosophical reflections on life. As part of the Vulgate, it has been widely read in the Latin West and remains a canonical text for biblical and medieval studies.

title: Revelation (Apocalypse)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Revelation (Apocalypse) from the Latin Vulgate is the final book of the New Testament. The Vulgate is the standard Latin Bible of the Western Church, and Revelation is a major apocalyptic text.

title: Romans
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Epistle to the Romans, found in the Latin Vulgate, is a central theological letter written by the Apostle Paul. It systematically explains the doctrines of salvation, grace, and faith. As a foundational text of Christian theology, it is of canonical importance in both religious and academic study.

title: Ruth
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Ruth in the Latin Vulgate is a cherished narrative of loyalty and redemption. This short biblical book is widely read and studied for its literary artistry and theological themes of kindness and divine providence.

title: Sirach (Ben Sira, Ecclesiasticus)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Sirach (Ecclesiasticus) from the Latin Vulgate is a wisdom book of the Old Testament Apocrypha, offering moral and practical advice. It was widely used in Christian moral instruction and is frequently cited in medieval Latin literature.

title: Tobit
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Tobit in the Vulgate, a deuterocanonical book rich in angelology and moral guidance. It has been valued for its narrative of faith and divine providence.

title: Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia) is a deuterocanonical book of philosophical and theological reflections.

title: Zechariah (Zachariah)
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: The Book of Zechariah (Zachariah) in the Latin Vulgate is a prophetic book from the Old Testament, containing messages about the restoration of Jerusalem and the coming of the Messiah. It is an important text for the study of prophecy and Jewish history in the Persian period, and is widely read.

title: Zephaniah
author: Latin Bible (Vulgate)
classification_notes: Zephaniah from the Vulgate.

title: Laus Pisonis
author: Laus Pisonis
classification_notes: The Laus Pisonis is a panegyric poem of disputed authorship.

title: mimus
author: Lentulus
classification_notes: Lentulus' mimus fragment, rare evidence for Roman mime.

title: palliatae
author: Licinius Imbrex
classification_notes: The palliatae (comedies in Greek dress) of Licinius Imbrex, a minor Republican playwright. Only 15 words survive, making this an obscure and fragmentary text. These scant remains are of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Roman comedy.

title: Bellum Civile
author: Lucan
classification_notes: Lucan's 'Bellum Civile' (or 'Pharsalia') is an epic poem on the civil war between Caesar and Pompey. A masterpiece of the Silver Age, it is innovative in its historical subject and rhetorical style.

title: carmina
author: Lucan
classification_notes: The poems (carmina) of Lucan beyond his epic 'Bellum Civile' survive only in minor fragments. While Lucan's epic is canonical, these scattered verses are of secondary interest, mainly for understanding his broader poetic output and the literary culture of the Neronian period.

title: carmina
author: Lucilius iunior
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Lucilius Iunior fl. 4-64; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Only 29 words survive from the carmina of Lucilius Iunior, the dedicatee of Seneca's Moral Epistles. These fragments, preserved in the Fragmenta Poetarum Latinorum, are too brief for meaningful literary analysis. The work is obscure, known only to specialists in Latin poetic fragments and Neronian literature.

title: Epitome Bell. Omn. Ann. DCC
author: Lucius Annaeus Florus
classification_notes: Florus's Epitome Bellorum Omnium Annorum DCC is a concise history of Rome's wars, popular as a summary of Roman military history.

title: Vergilius Orator an Poeta
author: Lucius Annaeus Florus
classification_notes: Florus' Vergilius Orator an Poeta is a brief rhetorical treatise debating whether Virgil should be classified primarily as an orator or a poet. It survives only as a fragment and is of interest to specialists in Latin rhetoric, literary criticism, and the reception of Virgil.

title: carmina
author: Lucius Annaeus Florus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Florus, Lucius Annaeus; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Only 15 words survive from the carmina of Lucius Annaeus Florus, a rhetorician and historian of the Imperial period. These fragments are preserved in the Fragmenta Poetarum Latinorum collection. The extant verses, while too brief for sustained literary analysis, are of interest to specialists in Latin poetic fragments and the history of the Latin epic and didactic traditions. The work's classification as poetry is based on the inherited metadata from Perseus.

title: carmina in Anthologia Latina
author: Lucius Annaeus Florus
classification_notes: Lucius Annaeus Florus' carmina, preserved in the Anthologia Latina, are short poems showcasing occasional verse. They are of interest for the study of Latin anthological traditions and minor imperial poetry.

title: epist. ad imperat. Hadrianum
author: Lucius Annaeus Florus
classification_notes: Only 13 words survive of an epistle purportedly by Lucius Annaeus Florus to the emperor Hadrian. The fragment is too brief to determine its content or context with certainty. It is of primarily antiquarian interest, noted in collections of Latin epistolary fragments.

title: Agamemnon
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Agamemnon is a major Roman tragedy, reflecting Stoic philosophy and Silver Latin style.

title: Apocolocyntosis
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Apocolocyntosis (The 'Pumpkinification' of Claudius) is a satirical Menippean work lampooning the deification of Emperor Claudius. It is a unique and influential piece of Latin literature, blending prose and verse to mock imperial politics. It is widely read as a key example of ancient satire and political humor.

title: De Beneficiis
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's 'De Beneficiis' is a substantial ethical treatise (46593 words) on the nature of giving and receiving benefits. A major Stoic work, it is widely read for its moral philosophy and insight into Roman social values.

title: De Clementia
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's De Clementia is a philosophical treatise on mercy, addressed to Nero, advocating for clemency as a virtue of good governance.

title: De Vita Patris
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's 'De Vita Patris' is a fragmentary biography of his father, Seneca the Elder. It is of interest to specialists in the Senecan family and the history of Roman biography.

title: Dialogi
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Dialogi, a collection of philosophical essays on Stoic ethics, are major works of Roman philosophy. They examine topics such as anger, tranquility, and providence, and remain widely read in Latin and translation.

title: Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's 'Epistulae Morales ad Lucilium' is a collection of philosophical letters on Stoic ethics. It is a canonical work of Latin philosophy, widely read for its moral insights and literary style.

title: Hercules Furens
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Hercules Furens is a Senecan tragedy, one of the few surviving Latin tragedies. It is widely studied for its Stoic themes and dramatic structure, placing it in the major popularity tier.

title: Hercules Oetaeus
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Hercules Oetaeus is one of the Senecan tragedies attributed to Seneca the Younger, though its authenticity is debated. It dramatizes the violent death and apotheosis of Hercules, employing the rhetorical and Stoic themes characteristic of the corpus.

title: Medea
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Medea is a powerful tragedy reworking Euripides' plot with Senecan rhetoric and Stoic themes, showcasing the playwright's dramatic skill.

title: Naturales Quaestiones
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Naturales Quaestiones is a comprehensive Stoic treatise on natural phenomena, ranging from meteorology to geography. It is a major work of Roman scientific literature and philosophical cosmology.

title: Octavia sp.
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: The Octavia is a Roman tragedy traditionally attributed to Seneca, though its authorship is disputed. It is the only surviving Roman historical drama (praetexta), dramatizing the fate of Nero's wife. As a major but not centrally canonical work, it is frequently studied within Latin drama and Senecan scholarship.

title: Oedipus
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Oedipus is a powerful tragedy based on the Theban myth, exemplifying Stoic themes and rhetorical intensity. It is a major work of Latin drama and influential in the Renaissance and modern theatre.

title: Phaedra
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Phaedra is a Roman tragedy based on the myth of Phaedra and Hippolytus. It is one of Seneca's most famous and frequently studied plays, known for its psychological intensity and rhetorical style. It is a major text in Latin drama and Senecan scholarship.

title: Phoenissae
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Phoenissae, centered on Theban mythology, is a powerful tragedy exploring themes of exile and fate, part of the Senecan tragic corpus.

title: Thyestes
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Thyestes is one of the most powerful and influential Latin tragedies, dramatizing the myth of Atreus and Thyestes with characteristic Stoic and rhetorical intensity. It is a central work in the Senecan corpus.

title: Troades
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
classification_notes: Seneca's Troades is a powerful tragedy on the fall of Troy, exploring themes of grief, fate, and cruelty, and is a key text in Roman Stoic drama.

title: e Cleanthe versus
author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Seneca, Lucius Annaeus ca. 4 B.C.-65 A.D; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Seneca's fragment of Cleanthes' verses.

title: De Arboribus
author: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella
classification_notes: Columella's De Arboribus, a treatise on tree cultivation, part of his larger agricultural work.

title: De Re Rustica
author: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella
classification_notes: Columella's De Re Rustica is a comprehensive treatise on Roman agriculture, covering crops, livestock, and estate management. It is a key source for ancient farming practices and rural life in the early Roman Empire.

title: Capitula
author: Lucretius
classification_notes: The Capitula of Lucretius are a set of chapter headings or summaries for his De Rerum Natura. They are transmitted with the poem and serve as a structural guide. While not a freestanding work, they are often included in editions and are of moderate interest for understanding the organization of Lucretius' Epicurean epic.

title: De Rerum Natura
author: Lucretius
classification_notes: Lucretius' De Rerum Natura is a didactic epic poem expounding Epicurean physics and ethics. It is a canonical work of Latin literature and philosophy, celebrated for its poetic power and its systematic presentation of a Hellenistic philosophical system.

title: fragmenta
author: Lucretius
classification_notes: Lucretius' 'De Rerum Natura' is a foundational didactic epic of Epicurean philosophy, central to Latin literature and widely studied across curricula. The fragmentary remains (66 words) represent a tiny portion of the original work, but the work's overall canonical status is clear.

title: palliatae
author: Luscius Lanuvinus
classification_notes: The palliatae (comedies in Greek dress) of Luscius Lanuvinus, a playwright from the Republican period. Only 15 words survive, making this an obscure and fragmentary text. These scant remains are of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Roman comedy.

title: iurisprudentia
author: M'. Manilius
classification_notes: A 77-word fragment from the 'iurisprudentia' of M'. Manilius, a Roman jurist. This brief excerpt is a specialist source for the study of early Roman legal thought and its textual tradition.

title: orationes
author: M. Aemilius Lepidus Porcina
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Lepidus Porcina, M. Aemilius (Marcus Aemilius) active 137 B.C.-125 B.C; perseus_editor=Meyer, Heinrich; perseus_year_published=1842; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of speeches (orationes) by the late Republican orator M. Aemilius Lepidus Porcina, preserved only in a few words. They offer scant evidence of his rhetorical activity.

title: De Vita Sua
author: M. Aemilius Scaurus
classification_notes: Scaurus' autobiographical fragment, a rare example of early Roman autobiography.

title: orationes
author: M. Aemilius Scaurus
classification_notes: Fragments of speeches by M. Aemilius Scaurus, a Roman politician of the 2nd century BCE. The surviving 73 words provide only a glimpse of his oratory.

title: iurisprudentia, fragmenta
author: M. Antistius Labeo
classification_notes: Fragments of the jurisprudential writings of M. Antistius Labeo, a prominent Augustan jurist known for his republican sympathies and legal innovation. These fragmenta (309 words) survive only in later citations. They are crucial for reconstructing the development of Roman jurisprudence in the early Empire and are studied by specialists in Roman law.

title: orationes
author: M. Antonius
classification_notes: Only 17 words survive from the orationes of M. Antonius (Mark Antony). As a historical figure, Antony was a major political player, but his speeches are almost entirely lost. The fragments are of interest primarily to specialists in Republican oratory and the history of the late Republic.

title: orationes
author: M. Antonius triumvir r. p. c.
classification_notes: These fragmentary 'orationes' by M. Antonius are remnants of his speeches. They are obscure texts, known only from indirect transmission. Their interest is limited to specialists in fragmentary Roman oratory and the history of the triumviral period.

title: carmen
author: M. Aurelius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Marcus Aurelius Emperor of Rome 121-180; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A 4-word fragment attributed to Marcus Aurelius, titled 'carmen'. The extreme brevity and generic title make identification uncertain; it may be a poetic fragment from the emperor, but the evidence is minimal.

title: orationes
author: M. Caelius Rufus
classification_notes: This entry collects fragments of speeches attributed to M. Caelius Rufus, a Roman orator and politician of the late Republic. The surviving fragments are very brief (169 words) and preserve little of Caelius's rhetorical style or argumentation. The work is of specialist interest, primarily to scholars of fragmentary Roman oratory.

title: oratio
author: M. Calidius
classification_notes: Only 31 words survive of an oratio by M. Calidius, a Roman orator of the late Republic. The fragment is too short to recover any rhetorical context. It is of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Latin oratory.

title: Ad Amicos Epistulae
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's letters to his friends (Ad Amicos Epistulae) are a collection of private correspondence from the second century AD, offering valuable insight into the language and culture of the Roman elite. They are a key source for the study of Latin epistolography and the Second Sophistic.

title: Ad Antoninum Pium Epistulae
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Ad Antoninum Pium Epistulae is a collection of letters by Fronto, a prominent orator and tutor to Marcus Aurelius. The correspondence provides insight into imperial court life and rhetorical education, of specialist interest in Latin epistolography.

title: Ad M. Antoninum Imp. Epist.
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's letters to Emperor Marcus Aurelius are an important collection of Imperial correspondence, revealing relations between a tutor and his imperial pupil.

title: Ad M. Caesarem et Invicem
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's correspondence with Marcus Aurelius, Ad M. Caesarem et Invicem, provides unique insight into the intellectual and rhetorical culture of the Antonine court. It is a vital source for Latin epistolography and the history of Latin rhetoric.

title: Ad Verum Imp. Epistulae
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's Ad Verum Imp. Epistulae, letters to Emperor Lucius Verus, reflecting imperial correspondence.

title: Additamentum Epist. Aceph.
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: The Additamentum Epist. Aceph. (Additions to the Headless Letters) by M. Cornelius Fronto forms part of his recovered correspondence. These are brief epistolary fragments that, while small in extent, contribute to the reconstruction of Fronto's letter collection and his intellectual exchange with the imperial court. The text is of specialist interest to scholars of Fronto, Latin epistolography, and the Antonine period.

title: Arion
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's 'Arion' is a fragmentary work, likely a rhetorical piece or declamation. It provides a glimpse into the literary activities of the Antonine orator and tutor to Marcus Aurelius.

title: De Bello Parthico
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's De Bello Parthico is a fragmentary historical work on the Parthian campaigns of the 2nd century AD. Only meager excerpts survive, offering limited insight into Fronto's historical writing; it is of interest mainly to scholars of Fronto and Roman historiography.

title: De Eloquentia
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's De Eloquentia, a treatise on oratory and style by the tutor of Marcus Aurelius.

title: De Feriis Alsiensibus
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's De Feriis Alsiensibus, a letter on the holidays at Alsium, part of his epistolary corpus.

title: De Nepote Amisso
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: De Nepote Amisso is a consolation speech by the orator Fronto on the loss of a grandson. It exemplifies the rhetorical practices of the Second Sophistic in Latin and offers insight into Fronto's style and personal concerns.

title: De Orationibus
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's De Orationibus, a rhetorical treatise by the leading Latin orator of the Antonine age. This work offers insight into the theory and practice of oratory as taught to Marcus Aurelius.

title: Laudes Fumi et Pulveris
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's Laudes Fumi et Pulveris is a playful rhetorical exercise (laus) typical of the Second Sophistic. It is of interest to scholars of Latin epistolography and imperial declamation.

title: Laudes Neglegentiae
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: A brief declamatory piece by the orator and letter-writer M. Cornelius Fronto, Laudes Neglegentiae is a rhetorical encomium of negligence, showcasing the Second Sophistic taste for paradoxical praise (adoxography). As a fragment surviving within Fronto's epistolary corpus, it offers insight into Imperial Latin rhetorical education and the stylistic preferences of the Antonine court. With only 368 words, it is a specialist text of interest primarily to scholars of Fronto and Latin declamation.

title: Principia Historiae
author: M. Cornelius Fronto
classification_notes: Fronto's 'Principia Historiae' is a fragmentary historical work on the Parthian wars of Lucius Verus. It is of interest to specialists in Roman imperial history and Fronto's literary circle.

classification_notes: Iunius Gracchanus's Commentarii, known from fragments, appear to have been historical-legal notes, shedding light on early Roman antiquarian scholarship.

title: tragoediae
author: M. Pacuvius
classification_notes: The tragoediae of M. Pacuvius survive in 2763 words of fragments, making him a key figure in early Roman tragedy. His work is crucial for understanding the development of Latin drama and its Greek models.

title: Carmen De Moribus
author: M. Porcius Cato
classification_notes: The 'Carmen De Moribus' is a didactic poem attributed to Cato the Elder, consisting of a single fragment (84 words) on moral conduct. It is primarily of interest to scholars of early Latin poetry and the transmission of ethical precepts.

title: De Agri Cultura
author: M. Porcius Cato
classification_notes: Cato the Elder's 'De Agri Cultura' is the oldest surviving Latin prose work. It is a practical manual on farming and estate management, reflecting early Roman values and economy. It is widely read as a source for Roman agriculture, daily life, and the origins of Latin technical writing.

author: M. Porcius Cato
classification_notes: Fragments of Cato's De Agri Cultura, the main work is a major agricultural manual.

title: De Medicina
author: M. Porcius Cato
classification_notes: De Medicina by Cato the Elder is a fragmentary work on medicine, known only from quotations. It is of specialist interest for early Roman medical practice.

classification_notes: Cato's De Re Militari is a fragmentary treatise on military affairs, known only from 109 words quoted by later authors. It was part of Cato the Elder's encyclopedic project. The work is of specialist interest for Roman military history and early Latin technical writing.

author: M. Porcius Cato

author: M. Porcius Cato

classification_notes: Cato the Elder's 'Origines' is a foundational work of Roman historiography, covering the early history of Rome and Italy. It survives only in fragments but was a seminal text.

title: Annales
author: M. Terentius Varro
classification_notes: This fragment of Varro's Annales is an extremely brief (17 words) relic of his lost historical epic or annalistic work. Varro was a prolific scholar, but his poetry is almost entirely lost. This entry is of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Latin literature and the history of Roman historiography.

title: De Lingua Latina
author: M. Terentius Varro
classification_notes: Varro's De Lingua Latina is a foundational work of Roman linguistic science, composed in the late Republic. It treats etymology, morphology, and syntax, surviving in six incomplete books that remain essential for Latin philology.

classification_notes: Fragments of Varro's De Vita Populi Romani, an antiquarian work on Roman customs and social history. The surviving excerpts, preserved by later grammarians, are valuable for reconstructing Varro's ethnographic interests and are studied by specialists in Roman antiquarianism.

classification_notes: Varro's Menippeae are Menippean satires surviving in fragments (7206 words). They are a unique blend of prose and verse, combining moral and literary criticism with scholarly learning, and are essential for understanding Varro's polymathic output and the satirical tradition.

title: Res Rusticae
author: M. Terentius Varro
classification_notes: Varro's Res Rusticae is a dialogue on agriculture divided into three books, covering farming, livestock, and villa management. It is an important source for Roman agricultural practices and rural life. While not as widely read as Vergil's Georgics, it is a key text for Roman economic history and technical Latin prose.

author: M. Terentius Varro
classification_notes: Varro's Res Urbanae is a fragment of a work on urban affairs by the prolific scholar.

author: M. Terentius Varro

title: De Notis Iuris
author: M. Valerius Probus
classification_notes: Probus's 'De Notis Iuris' is a treatise on legal abbreviations and notation. It is a specialist work for Roman legal philology and the study of juristic shorthand, of interest primarily to historians of Roman law and textual transmission.

title: Vita Persii
author: M. Valerius Probus
classification_notes: Probus's Vita Persii, a brief biography of the satirist Persius. As a source for the life of the poet, it is important for understanding the background of his satires.

title: Academica
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Academica is a philosophical dialogue on the theory of knowledge, presenting the views of the Academic Skeptics and the Stoics. Written in 45 BCE, only portions of the original two-book version survive. It is a key text for understanding Hellenistic epistemology and Cicero's philosophical project. Commonly read in advanced Latin and philosophy courses, the Academica is a major work for the study of Roman Stoicism and Skepticism.

title: Arati Phaenomena
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Arati Phaenomena, a translation of Aratus' astronomical poem, is important for the reception of Hellenistic science in Latin. It also influenced later Latin astronomical poetry.

title: Arati Prognostica

title: Brutus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Brutus is a dialogue on the history of Roman oratory, surveying notable speakers from the early Republic to Cicero's own time. It is a key work for the study of Roman rhetoric and literary history.

title: Cato Maior de Senectute
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Cato Maior de Senectute is a philosophical dialogue on old age, written in 44 BCE. It is a key work of Latin philosophical literature, demonstrating Cicero's adaptation of Greek themes and his rhetorical skill. The work is widely read in Latin curricula.

title: Commentarii Causarum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Commentarii Causarum by Cicero, known only from fragments, likely dealt with legal cases. It is of specialist interest within Cicero's rhetorical and legal corpus.

title: De Divinatione
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's 'De Divinatione' is a major philosophical dialogue on the nature of prophecy and divination. It is a key text for understanding Roman religious thought and Stoic epistemology, widely read by scholars of ancient philosophy.

title: De Domo Sua
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's defense speech De Domo Sua, delivered after his return from exile. It is a rhetorical masterpiece, combining legal argument with political invective and personal narrative.

title: De Fato
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's 'De Fato' is a philosophical dialogue on the problem of fate and free will, reflecting Stoic and Academic debates. Written in 44 BCE, it is part of Cicero's series of philosophical works and remains a key text for Roman Stoicism.

title: De Finibus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Finibus Bonorum et Malorum is a philosophical dialogue on the nature of the highest good and the ultimate end of human action, systematically presenting and critiquing Epicurean, Stoic, and Academic views. It is a central text for understanding Hellenistic ethics and Roman philosophical literature.

title: De Haruspicum Responso

classification_notes: Cicero's 'De Haruspicum Responso' is a speech delivered in the Senate concerning a response from the haruspices. It is a common work within the Ciceronian corpus, studied for its political and religious context.

title: De Inventione
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Inventione is an early and influential treatise on rhetoric, composed while he was still a young man. It systematically discusses the invention of arguments for speeches, drawing on earlier Greek rhetorical theory. The work remains a key source for understanding Roman rhetorical education and practice, and is widely studied.

title: De Iure Civ. in Artem Redig.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: A fragmentary work by Cicero, De Iure Civ. in Artem Redig. (On the Civil Law Systematized), represented by only 24 words. This is a minimal remnant of Cicero's attempt to systematize Roman civil law. The text is obscure and studied only by the most specialized scholars of Roman legal history and Ciceronian fragments.

title: De Lege Agraria

These orations are a key example of Cicero's political rhetoric and his defense of the established order, widely studied for their argumentative strategy and style.

title: De Legibus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Legibus is a philosophical dialogue on the ideal laws and natural law, complementing his De Re Publica and influential in legal theory.

title: De Natura Deorum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Natura Deorum is a key philosophical dialogue on theology.

title: De Officiis
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Officiis is a foundational work of Stoic ethics, addressing moral duties and practical conduct, influencing Western moral philosophy profoundly.

title: De Optimo Genere Oratorum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's 'De Optimo Genere Oratorum' is a short rhetorical treatise on the ideal orator. It is a common work within the Ciceronian corpus, studied for its contribution to rhetorical theory.

title: De Oratore
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Oratore is a foundational dialogue on rhetoric, set in 91 BC. It is a central text of Latin prose and rhetorical theory, widely studied for its stylistic excellence and philosophical depth. As a canonical work, it is essential reading in classics and rhetoric curricula.

title: De Partitione Oratoria
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Partitione Oratoria, a dialogue on the divisions of rhetoric. This work systematically outlines the components of oratorical theory and practice.

title: De Provinciis Consularibus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
