classification_notes: Cicero's speech De Provinciis Consularibus argues for the assignment of provinces to the consuls of 56 BC. It is a key example of his oratory and provides insight into late Republican political maneuvering.

title: De Republica
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's De Republica is a Platonic dialogue on the ideal state, surviving in fragments and excerpts. It is a cornerstone of Roman political thought.

title: Epistulae ad Atticum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Johnston, Harold Whetstone; perseus_year_published=1910; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's letters to Atticus are a monumental corpus of personal and political correspondence from the Late Republic. They are indispensable for understanding Roman history, politics, and Cicero's private thoughts.

title: Epistulae ad Brutum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Epistulae ad Brutum are a collection of letters to and from Marcus Junius Brutus, offering key insight into late Republican politics and Cicero's personal relationships. They are major sources for the period.

title: Epistulae ad Familiares
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Johnston, Harold Whetstone; perseus_year_published=1910; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's Epistulae ad Familiares is a collection of personal and political letters, offering invaluable insight into late Republican Roman society, politics, and rhetoric. These letters are a cornerstone for understanding Cicero's relationships and the turbulent events of his time.

title: Epistulae ad Quintum Fratrem
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Epistulae ad Quintum Fratrem is a collection of letters to his brother Quintus, offering an intimate view of Cicero's personal and political life. These letters are a crucial source for the history of the late Roman Republic. They are widely studied by historians and classicists.

title: Facete Dicta
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Facete Dicta is a collection of reported witticisms, attributed to him but possibly a compilation. It provides insight into Roman humor and Cicero's reputation as a wit, and is of interest to students of Roman social history and rhetoric.

title: Hortensius
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Hortensius, a lost protreptic dialogue urging the pursuit of philosophy, survives only in fragments. It was influential in antiquity and is known to have inspired Augustine.

title: In Catilinam
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's four speeches against Catiline are among the most famous works of Roman oratory, denouncing the conspiracy with passionate rhetorical force and political urgency.

title: In Pisonem
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's invective against Piso, a key example of Late Republican political oratory.

title: In Q. Caecilium
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Peterson, William; perseus_year_published=1917; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's speech In Q. Caecilium (Against Caecilius) is a preliminary action in the prosecution of Verres, where Cicero argues that Caecilius should not be allowed to prosecute instead. It is a powerful example of Ciceronian rhetoric and a key text for understanding Roman legal procedure. It is widely studied.

title: In Sallustium sp.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: The 'In Sallustium' is an invective speech transmitted under Cicero's name but widely considered spurious, likely a later rhetorical exercise. Its attribution remains disputed, but it provides insight into the reception of Ciceronian style.

title: In Vatinium
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Peterson, William; perseus_year_published=1909 | 1911; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's In Vatinium, a speech attacking P. Vatinius during a trial. A sharp example of Ciceronian invective, it showcases his rhetorical skill in cross-examination and character assassination.

title: In Verrem
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Johnston, Harold Whetstone | Peterson, William; perseus_year_published=1910 | 1917; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's In Verrem comprises the speeches delivered in 70 BCE against Gaius Verres, the corrupt former governor of Sicily. These orations are a cornerstone of Roman legal rhetoric and a major source for the study of Late Republican oratory, provincial administration, and Roman law. Extending over 103,000 words, the Verrines are widely read and cited across curricula in Latin literature, history, and rhetoric, and they remain a central text in the Ciceronian corpus.

title: Laelius de Amicitia
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Laelius de Amicitia (On Friendship) is a philosophical dialogue exploring the nature and value of friendship. Set in the late Republic, it is a key text in Roman moral philosophy and has been widely read for its humane and practical wisdom.

title: Lucullus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Lucullus (also known as Academica Priora), a key philosophical work on epistemology.

title: Orator
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Orator is a treatise on the ideal orator and the three styles of rhetoric. It is a central text in Latin rhetorical theory and a key source for understanding Cicero's own oratorical practice and the broader Roman rhetorical tradition.

title: Paradoxa Stoicorum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Paradoxa Stoicorum is a short work presenting Stoic paradoxes in a popular style, illustrating Cicero's philosophical interests.

title: Philippicae
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Clark, Albert Curtis | Johnston, Harold Whetstone; perseus_year_published=1910 | 1918; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's 'Philippicae' are a series of fourteen speeches against Mark Antony, among the most famous examples of Roman oratory. They are central to the study of Latin rhetoric, late Republican politics, and Cicero's career.

title: Post Reditum ad Populum
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Peterson, William; perseus_year_published=1909 | 1911; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Post Reditum ad Populum is a speech by Cicero delivered after his return from exile. It is a key example of late Republican oratory and is widely studied, placing it in the major popularity tier.

title: Post Reditum in Senatu
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Post Reditum in Senatu' is a speech delivered after his return from exile in 57 BCE. It is a key example of his oratory and provides insight into the political struggles of the late Republic.

title: Pro Archia

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Archia is a celebrated defense speech for the poet Archias, arguing for the value of literature and citizenship. It is a staple of Latin rhetorical studies and a key document for Roman cultural history.

title: Pro Balbo
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Pro Balbo' is a forensic speech defending the citizenship rights of L. Cornelius Balbus. It provides insights into Roman citizenship law and oratorical strategy, and is a commonly read text in Ciceronian studies.

title: Pro Caecina
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Clark, Albert Curtis; perseus_year_published=1909; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Caecina is a forensic speech exemplifying Roman legal oratory.

title: Pro Caelio
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Caelio is a masterful defense speech for Marcus Caelius Rufus on charges of violence and attempted poisoning. It is a set piece of Roman oratory, celebrated for its wit and rhetorical brilliance.

title: Pro Cluentio
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Clark, Albert Curtis; perseus_year_published=1905 | 1908; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Cluentio is a celebrated speech from 66 BC defending Aulus Cluentius Habitus on a charge of murder. It is one of Cicero's longest surviving speeches and a masterwork of rhetorical strategy. It is a major text for the study of Roman law and Ciceronian oratory.

title: Pro Flacco
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Clark, Albert Curtis; perseus_year_published=1909; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Flacco is a defense speech from 59 BCE, delivered on behalf of Lucius Valerius Flaccus on charges of extortion. It is a significant example of Ciceronian oratory and provincial administration.

title: Pro Fonteio
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Fonteio is a fragmentary speech in defense of a Roman governor accused of extortion. It is an important example of Cicero's forensic oratory and provides insight into Republican provincial administration and legal procedure.

title: Pro Lege Manilia

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Pro Lege Manilia' (also known as 'De Imperio Cn. Pompei') is a speech from 66 BCE supporting the command of Pompey against Mithridates. It is a classic example of deliberative oratory and a key source for late Republican history.

title: Pro Ligario

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Ligario is a short judicial speech from 46 BC defending Quintus Ligarius before Caesar. It is a masterpiece of Ciceronian persuasion and clemency rhetoric. It is commonly taught in Latin courses and is a standard text for intermediate Latin readers.

title: Pro Marcello

classification_notes: Pro Marcello is a celebrated speech by Cicero praising Caesar's clemency. It is a staple of Latin rhetoric and widely studied, earning major popularity.

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Murena is a defense speech for Lucius Licinius Murena, prosecuted for electoral bribery. It is a classic example of Cicero's rhetorical skill, combining legal argument with political acumen and humor.

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Pro Plancio' is a forensic speech delivered in 54 BCE, defending his former quaestor. It offers insight into Roman politics and oratory, and is commonly read as a key example of Ciceronian rhetoric.

classification_notes: Cicero's speech Pro Roscio Comoedo, a masterful example of Roman oratory.

title: Pro Quinctio
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Quinctio, his earliest surviving speech (81 BCE), is a foundational text of Roman forensic oratory. It establishes Cicero's rhetorical method and is widely studied for its argumentative structure and historical context.

title: Pro Rabirio Perduellionis Reo

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Pro Rabirio Perduellionis Reo' is a speech in defense of Gaius Rabirius on a charge of treason. It is a common work within the Ciceronian corpus, studied for its legal and historical context.

title: Pro Rabirio Postumo

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Rabirio Postumo is a forensic speech defending a Roman knight on charges of extortion. It is a well-known example of Ciceronian oratory, frequently studied for its rhetorical technique and historical context.

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Rege Deiotaro is a judicial speech defending the Galatian king Deiotarus. It is a well-known example of Ciceronian oratory and is studied for its rhetorical strategy and historical context.

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro S. Roscio Amerino, his first major criminal defense, secured the acquittal of Sextus Roscius on a charge of parricide. It is a landmark speech in Cicero's career, demonstrating his political and oratorical skill.

classification_notes: Cicero's 'Pro Scauro' is a forensic speech in defense of Marcus Aemilius Scaurus. It is a significant example of Ciceronian oratory and Roman legal practice. It is a major work within the corpus of Cicero's speeches.

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Sestio is a judicial speech delivered in 56 BC in defense of Publius Sestius on charges of public violence. It is a key text for understanding Roman political history and Ciceronian oratory. It is widely read in Latin classes and scholarship.

classification_notes: Cicero's Pro Tullio, a fragmentary speech defending a property case. It demonstrates early Ciceronian forensic oratory and his handling of civil law.

title: Rhetorica ad Herennium sp.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: The 'Rhetorica ad Herennium' is a Latin rhetorical treatise, traditionally attributed to Cicero but now considered spurious. It is a major work in the history of rhetoric, widely used as a textbook in the Renaissance and beyond.

title: Timaeus
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's translation of Plato's Timaeus is a landmark in the transmission of Greek philosophy into Latin. It is a key text for understanding Roman philosophical engagement.

title: Topica
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's Topica, a rhetorical and logical treatise on the art of finding arguments, was written in 44 BCE. It is an important work for Roman jurisprudence and Cicero's adaptation of Greek rhetorical theory.

title: Tusculanae Disputationes
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's 'Tusculanae Disputationes' is a series of five dialogues on happiness, pain, grief, and virtue. Written in 45 BCE, it is a cornerstone of Roman Stoic philosophy and a major influence on later thought.

title: carmina, fragmenta
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Cicero, Marcus Tullius; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Cicero's poetic fragments, including parts of his epic poems on his own consulship and other subjects, survive from quotations. Though Cicero was primarily an orator and philosopher, his poetic works were highly regarded in antiquity. This collection preserves about 1857 words of his verse.

title: epistula ad Octavianum sp.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Spurious letter to Octavian attributed to Cicero, reflecting later rhetorical practice.

title: epistulae, fragmenta
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: These fragmentary letters of Cicero, surviving only in excerpts, offer supplementary evidence for his epistolary corpus. They are of specialist interest for Ciceronian studies.

title: incertorum librorum fragmenta
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Fragments from uncertain books of Cicero, preserved without clear attribution to specific works. They represent minor textual remnants of Cicero's vast output.

title: orationum deperditarum frr.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: Cicero's 'orationum deperditarum frr.' are fragments of lost speeches. While fragmentary, they are important for reconstructing Cicero's oratorical corpus and late Republican politics, and are of significant scholarly interest.

title: orationum incertarum frr.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: These fragments of Cicero's lost orations ('orationum incertarum frr.') are crucial for reconstructing his full rhetorical corpus. They offer glimpses into cases and speeches otherwise unknown, warranting specialist study.

title: philosophicorum librorum frr.
author: Marcus Tullius Cicero
classification_notes: These 'philosophicorum librorum frr.' are fragments of Cicero's lost philosophical works. They are of specialist interest for the reconstruction of Cicero's philosophical output and the history of Roman philosophy.

title: Lupercalia
author: Marianus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Marianus; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Lupercalia by Marianus is a fragmentary poem on the Lupercal festival. It is of obscure interest, known only from a brief citation.

title: Epigrammata
author: Martial
classification_notes: Martial's Epigrammata is a major collection of satirical and occasional epigrams, widely read for its wit and social commentary. It is a canonical work of Latin poetry, frequently studied in curricula and scholarship.

title: Spectacula
author: Martial
classification_notes: Martial's Spectacula (Liber de Spectaculis) is his first published collection of epigrams, celebrating the inaugural games of the Colosseum. It is a vital source for Flavian spectacle and epigrammatic art.

title: mimi
author: Marullus
classification_notes: Exceedingly brief fragments of mimi (mime plays) by Marullus, preserved by later grammarians. The content is too scanty to assess their literary quality, and they are of interest only to specialists in Roman popular drama and fragments.

title: Commentarius in Artem Donati
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius's commentary on Donatus's Ars Grammatica is a central work of late antique grammatical education, expounding on the standard Latin grammar textbook and influencing medieval linguistic study.

title: De Centum Metris
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius' De Centum Metris, a technical treatise on Latin meters. It is an important source for Late Antique metrics and was used widely in medieval education.

title: De Finalibus
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius's De Finalibus is a grammatical treatise on the endings of Latin words, focusing on quantity and syllable weight. It is a technical but important work for understanding Late Latin grammatical theory. It is studied by specialists in Latin metrics and the history of grammar.

title: De Metris Horatianis
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius' De Metris Horatianis is a short treatise on the meters used by Horace. This technical work by the celebrated late antique commentator on Virgil provides an important witness to ancient metrical theory and the teaching of Horace's poetry. The work is of specialist interest to scholars of Latin metrics and Servius' scholarly output.

title: In Vergilii Aeneidos Libros
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius's commentary on Virgil's Aeneid is a monumental work of late antique scholarship, preserving grammatical, historical, and mythological learning essential for understanding Virgil.

title: In Vergilii Bucolicon Librum
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius' commentary on Virgil's Eclogues, a foundational work of Virgilian scholarship and late antique grammar.

title: In Vergilii Georgicon Libros
author: Maurus Servius Honoratus
classification_notes: Servius' commentary on Virgil's Georgics is an essential work of late antique scholarship. It provides valuable grammatical, historical, and mythological interpretation, widely used by students of Virgil.

title: Mimi Poetarum Incertorum
author: Mimi Poetarum Incertorum
classification_notes: Fragments of mimes from anonymous Roman poets. These brief excerpts represent the popular genre of mime, a form of comic performance in the Republican period.

title: fragmenta dubia
author: Mimi Poetarum Incertorum
classification_notes: These dubious fragments from uncertain poets of the Latin mime tradition survive in minimal form, offering little secure textual evidence.

title: Atellanae
author: Mummius
classification_notes: Mummius's Atellanae are fragments of Atellan farces, a genre of Italian folk comedy. The surviving 37 words offer only a glimpse into this early dramatic tradition.

title: Ilias
author: Naevius

classification_notes: Naevius's 'Ilias' is a fragmentary Latin epic on the Trojan War. It is an early example of Roman epic, of specialist interest for the development of Latin literature and its relation to Greek models.

title: Ilias
author: Ninnius Crassus

classification_notes: Ninnius Crassus's 'Ilias' is a fragmentary Latin translation or adaptation of the Iliad. It is an obscure text, known only from a few fragments. Its interest is limited to specialists in fragmentary Latin epic and the reception of Homer.

title: Atellanae
author: Novius
classification_notes: Novius' Atellanae comprises fragments of Roman Atellan farce, a genre of rustic comic performance. Though only fragments survive, they illuminate early Roman theatrical traditions and vernacular humor.

title: Antibucolica
author: Numitorius
classification_notes: Numitorius' Antibucolica is a fragment (24 words) of a response or parody of Vergil's Eclogues. It is a rare example of ancient literary criticism and response, but is so fragmentary as to be mostly a curiosity.

title: Amores

classification_notes: Ovid's Amores is a collection of elegiac love poems that revolutionized Roman erotic poetry, combining wit, passion, and literary playfulness.

title: Ars Amatoria
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Ars Amatoria is a didactic elegiac poem offering ironic advice on love and seduction. It is one of the most famous and controversial works of Augustan poetry, central to Ovid's corpus and to the study of Latin erotic elegy.

title: Epicedion Drusi sp.
author: Ovid
classification_notes: The Epicedion Drusi (Consolatio ad Liviam), a poem mourning the death of Drusus, often attributed to Ovid but of uncertain authorship.

title: Epistulae (vel Heroides)
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Epistulae (Heroides) are a collection of poetic letters by Ovid, written from mythological heroines to their lovers. They are a canonical work of Latin poetry, widely read and studied.

title: Epistulae ex Ponto
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1904 | 1922; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters from the Black Sea) is a collection of elegiac poems written during his exile. They are letters expressing his longing for Rome and his desperate pleas for recall. The work is a major source for understanding Ovid's exile and is widely studied for its literary and historical value.

title: Fasti
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Courtney, Edward | Ehwald, Rudolf | Frazer, James GeorgeSir; perseus_year_published=1904 | 1924 | 1931 | 1978; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Fasti is a didactic poem on the Roman calendar, explaining festivals and their mythological and historical origins. Written in elegiac couplets, it is a major source for Roman religion and tradition. Though unfinished, it is a highly valued and widely studied work of Latin poetry.

title: Halieutica sp.
author: Ovid
classification_notes: Ovid's Halieutica is a didactic poem on fishing, of debated authenticity.

title: Ibis
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1889 | 1904 | 1922; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's 'Ibis' is a complex curse poem against an unnamed enemy, blending elegiac meter with learned mythological and geographical allusions. It is a commonly read work in the Ovidian corpus, studied for its poetic vitriol and erudition.

title: Medea
author: Ovid
classification_notes: Ovid's tragedy Medea, now lost except for 14 words. This play was highly regarded in antiquity (Tacitus, Quintilian) but only the briefest fragments survive. The loss is significant for the history of Latin tragedy. The fragments are of specialist interest to scholars of Ovid and Roman drama, and the work is a common reference in studies of the reception of the Medea myth.

title: Medicamina Faciei Femineae
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Medicamina Faciei Femineae (Cosmetics for the Female Face) is a didactic elegiac poem on beauty treatments, surviving only in fragmentary form (approximately 100 lines). It is a unique text in Latin literature, blending practical advice with Ovid's characteristic wit and erotic poetry. Though incomplete, the poem is commonly read and studied for its insights into Roman cosmetics, gender, and Ovid's poetics.

title: Metamorphoses
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin | Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf | Knapp, Charles; perseus_year_published=1905 | 1922 | 1923; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Metamorphoses is a Latin narrative epic of over 250 myths unified by the theme of transformation. It is a cornerstone of Western literature, profoundly influencing art, literature, and culture. Its immense scope and artistry make it one of the most important and widely read works from classical antiquity.

title: Nux sp.
author: Ovid
classification_notes: The Nux (The Walnut Tree) is a Latin elegiac poem attributed to Ovid but of disputed authorship. It personifies a walnut tree lamenting its maltreatment. The poem is commonly included in Ovidian dubia and is read by students of Augustan poetry and attribution studies.

title: Remedia Amoris
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Remedia Amoris, a didactic elegy offering cures for love, is a companion to the Ars Amatoria. It is widely read for its wit and Ovidian sophistication.

title: Tristia
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf; perseus_year_published=1904 | 1922; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Ovid's Tristia (Sorrows) is a collection of elegiac poems written from his exile on the Black Sea. These poems offer a poignant and personal account of loss and longing, and are a major source for the circumstances of Ovid's relegation. The Tristia is widely read and studied, forming a canonical part of Ovid's exile poetry and Augustan literature.

title: carmina, fragmenta
author: Ovid
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin | Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Ovid 43 B.C.-17 or 18 A.D; perseus_editor=Ehwald, Rudolf | Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1924 | 1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: These fragments from the poems of Ovid, collected by modern editors, preserve traces of lost works by the Augustan poet. Despite their minimal word count, they offer valuable evidence for Ovid's literary range beyond the surviving corpus.

title: iurisprudentia, fragmenta
author: P. Alfenus Varus
classification_notes: These fragmenta of iurisprudentia by Alfenus Varus are minor legal fragments. They are of obscure interest, known only to historians of Roman law.

title: iurisprudentia
author: P. Aufidius Namusa
classification_notes: A fragment of legal writing by P. Aufidius Namusa, a jurist of the Augustan age; survives in a single citation.

title: oratio
author: P. Cannutius
classification_notes: A fragment of a speech by P. Cannutius, a minor orator of the Late Republic; known only from brief citations.

title: orationes
author: P. Clodius Pulcher
classification_notes: Fragments of Clodius Pulcher's orations.

title: oratio
author: P. Cominius
classification_notes: P. Cominius' oration fragment.

title: oratio
author: P. Cornelius Dolabella
classification_notes: A three-word fragment of a speech by P. Cornelius Dolabella, a contemporary of Cicero. The content is entirely lost, making this a negligible text for all but the most specialized prosopographical research.

title: orationes
author: P. Cornelius Scipio Aemilianus Africanus minor
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Scipio Aemilianus, P. Cornelius (Publius Cornelius) Africanus minor 185 B.C. or 184 B.C.-129 B.C; perseus_editor=Meyer, Heinrich; perseus_year_published=1842; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of speeches by Scipio Aemilianus, preserved primarily in later grammatical and historical sources, provide glimpses of his oratorical style. They are of interest mainly to specialists in Roman Republican oratory and fragmentary rhetorical texts.

title: oratio
author: P. Cornelius Scipio Africanus maior
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Scipio Africanus ca. 236-183 B.C; perseus_editor=Meyer, Heinrich; perseus_year_published=1842; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A single fragment of a speech by Scipio Africanus the Elder, preserved by later sources. It provides minimal evidence of his oratory and is of interest only to specialists in early Republican oratory and fragmentary texts.

title: orationes
author: P. Cornelius Scipio Nasica Serapio
classification_notes: Scipio Nasica's orations survive only in fragments, offering glimpses of early Roman oratory during the Republic.

title: fragmentum
author: P. Mucius Scaevola
classification_notes: A tiny fragment attributed to P. Mucius Scaevola, possibly from a legal or oratorical context; the title 'fragmentum' gives no further indication.

title: grammatica
author: P. Nigidius Figulus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Nigidius Figulus, Publius -45 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino; perseus_year_published=1907; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Nigidius Figulus' grammatical fragments preserve elements of his scholarly work on Latin language and etymology. A contemporary of Cicero, he was a polymath whose grammatical writings are valued by philologists.

title: Achilleis
author: P. Papinius (Publius Papinius) Statius
classification_notes: Statius's Achilleis is an unfinished epic on the life of Achilles, notable for its sophisticated style and mythological treatment, a major work of Flavian epic.

title: De Bello Germanico (fragment)
author: P. Papinius (Publius Papinius) Statius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Statius, P. Papinius (Publius Papinius); perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A fragment of Statius' De Bello Germanico, an epic poem on Domitian's German campaigns. Only a single line survives, providing scant evidence for this lost work; it is of interest only to specialists in Flavian epic and fragmentary Latin poetry.

title: Silvae
author: P. Papinius (Publius Papinius) Statius
classification_notes: Statius's Silvae, a collection of occasional poems celebrating patrons and events, a major work of Flavian poetry.

title: Thebais
author: P. Papinius (Publius Papinius) Statius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Statius, P. Papinius (Publius Papinius); perseus_editor=Klinnert, Thomas C.; perseus_year_published=1973; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Statius' Thebais is a major Flavian epic retelling the myth of the Seven Against Thebes. It is widely studied for its literary artistry, intertextuality with Virgil, and influence on later European literature.

title: praetextae
author: P. Pomponius Secundus
classification_notes: Pomponius Secundus' praetextae (historical dramas) survive only in fragments. As a rare example of Roman historical drama, the work is of specialist interest for the study of Republican and early Imperial theatrical genres.

title: tragoediae
author: P. Pomponius Secundus
classification_notes: The tragedies of P. Pomponius Secundus, a 1st-century CE Roman dramatist, survive only in scant fragments. Despite the loss of the complete plays, these remnants attest to his engagement with the tragic genre in the early Imperial period, though the brevity of the corpus limits literary analysis.

title: Schemata Lexeos
author: P. Rutilius Lupus
classification_notes: P. Rutilius Lupus's 'Schemata Lexeos' is a Latin rhetorical treatise on figures of speech, drawing on Greek sources. It is a specialist work, of interest primarily to scholars of ancient rhetoric and literary theory.

title: De Vita Sua
author: P. Rutilius Rufus
classification_notes: Rutilius Rufus' De Vita Sua is an autobiographical work surviving only in fragments, providing insight into Roman self-representation.

title: oratio
author: P. Saturius
classification_notes: This fragmentary 'oratio' by P. Saturius is a very brief remnant of a speech. It is an obscure text, known only from indirect transmission. Its interest is limited to specialists in fragmentary Roman oratory.

title: carmina
author: P. Terentius Varro Atacinus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin | Latin language--Grammar--Early works to 1500; perseus_author=Varro Atacinus, P. Terentius (Publius Terentius) 82 B.C.-ca. 35 B.C; perseus_editor=Funaioli, Gino | Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1907 | 1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Fragments of Varro Atacinus's poems, an important but fragmentary epic and didactic poet of the Late Republic.

title: Aeneis
author: P. Vergilius Maro (Virgil)
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin | Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Virgil; perseus_editor=Greenough, J. B. (James Bradstreet) | Knapp, Charles | Mynors, Roger Aubrey Baskerville; perseus_year_published=1881-84 | 1900 | 1923 | 1969; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Virgil's Aeneid, the national epic of Rome, is the foundational text of Latin literature. It has been continuously studied, translated, and adapted, and remains central to the Western canon.

title: Eclogae
author: P. Vergilius Maro (Virgil)
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin | Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Virgil; perseus_editor=Greenough, J. B. (James Bradstreet) | Mynors, Roger Aubrey Baskerville | Page, T.E. (Thomas Ethelbert); perseus_year_published=1881-84 | 1898 | 1900 | 1969; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Virgil's 'Eclogae' (Eclogues) are a collection of pastoral poems, a foundational work of Latin literature. They are a canonical text, central to the study of Augustan poetry and the pastoral tradition.

title: Georgica
author: P. Vergilius Maro (Virgil)
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Didactic poetry, Latin | Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Virgil; perseus_editor=Greenough, J. B. (James Bradstreet) | Mynors, Roger Aubrey Baskerville | Page, T.E. (Thomas Ethelbert); perseus_year_published=1898 | 1900 | 1969; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Virgil's 'Georgica' is a didactic poem on agriculture, a masterpiece of Latin literature. It is a canonical work, central to the study of Augustan poetry and its influence on later European literature.

title: epigrammation
author: Papinius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Papinius 1. Jh. v. Chr./1. Jh. n. Chr; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Only 27 words survive of Papinius' epigrammation (a collection of epigrams). The author is otherwise unknown. These fragments are too slight to characterize the work substantively, but they belong to the tradition of Latin epigram. The text is of interest only to specialists in fragmentary Latin poetry.

title: carmina
author: Parthenius Presbyter
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Parthenius Constantinopolitanus 5. Jh. n. Chr; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The 'carmina' of Parthenius Presbyter, a late antique poet, survive in a 94-word fragment. These remains are of specialist interest for the study of Christian Latin poetry in the fifth century.

title: oratio
author: Passienus Crispus
classification_notes: This oration by Passienus Crispus survives only in a fragment of 9 words. As a minor piece of imperial oratory, it is of interest only to specialists in Roman rhetoric and fragmentary speech traditions.

title: carmina
author: Paulus Quaestor
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Paulus Quaestor; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The Carmina of Paulus Quaestor survive only in a fragment of 22 words, preserved in later anthologies. The author is otherwise obscure, possibly a late Latin poet of the imperial period. The surviving lines do not permit firm literary or thematic classification.

title: Saturae
author: Persius
classification_notes: Persius' Saturae are a collection of six satires in hexameter verse, known for their Stoic moralizing and dense style. They are a major work of Roman satire, frequently studied for their philosophical content and literary technique.

title: Satyrica
author: Petronius Arbiter
classification_notes: Petronius' Satyrica, a comic-satirical novel in prose and verse. A unique survival of Roman Menippean satire, it vividly depicts the extravagant banquet of Trimalchio and the mores of Neronian society.

title: Satyrica, fragmenta
author: Petronius Arbiter
classification_notes: Petronius's Satyrica survives only in fragments, but it is a masterpiece of Roman satire and a precursor to the novel. The work offers a vivid portrayal of Neronian society and is highly valued for its literary innovation.

title: Fabulae Aesopiae
author: Phaedrus
classification_notes: Phaedrus' Fabulae Aesopiae is a collection of verse fables adapting Aesopic material into Latin iambic senarii. It is commonly read for its moral content and as a key text in the fable tradition.

title: Fabularum Appendix
author: Phaedrus
classification_notes: The 'Fabularum Appendix' is a collection of fables transmitted alongside those of Phaedrus. Though its authorship is uncertain, it expands the Phaedrian corpus and is important for the study of the fable genre.

title: De medicina, versio Latina
author: Philumenus medicus
classification_notes: Philumenus's medical treatise, here in a Latin translation, represents the transmission of Greek medical knowledge into the Latin West. It deals with various ailments and remedies, and is of specialist interest for the history of medicine and the reception of Galenic and Hippocratic traditions in late antiquity.

title: De Animalibus
author: Pompeius Trogus
classification_notes: A fragment of Pompeius Trogus's De Animalibus, part of his lost natural history; survives only in brief excerpts.

title: Historiae Philippicae
author: Pompeius Trogus
classification_notes: The Epitome of the Philippic Histories by Pompeius Trogus, composed during the Augustan period, is a universal history centered on the Macedonian empire. The surviving brief text preserves an overview of Trogus's lost larger work, providing valuable historical narrative.

title: epigramma
author: Pompilius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Pompilius fl. 100 B.C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: The epigramma of Pompilius, a short fragmentary poem from the Late Republic, is preserved only in a few words. Its attribution to the otherwise obscure poet Pompilius is secure but the work itself is of minimal extent and significance within the Latin poetic corpus.

title: tragoedia
author: Pompilius
classification_notes: Pompilius' tragedy fragment, only one word, is all that remains of an early Roman tragedy.

title: De Chorographia
author: Pomponius Mela
classification_notes: De Chorographia is a geographical treatise by Pomponius Mela, the earliest surviving Latin work on geography. It is of specialist interest but has moderate recognition among classical geographers.

title: Comment. in Hor. Artem Poet.
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Commentary on Horace's Ars Poetica by Pomponius Porphyrio, a key source for ancient Horatian exegesis.

title: Comment. in Hor. Carm. Saec.
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Pomponius Porphyrio's commentary on Horace's Carmen Saeculare is a grammatical and exegetical work that explains the text's historical and literary context. It is a valuable resource for scholars of Horace and ancient scholarship.

title: Commentum in Horati Carmina
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Porphyrio's commentary on Horace's Carmina is a key early Latin scholia, providing grammatical and historical explanations. It is an important source for the reception of Horace and for ancient scholarship on Latin poetry, widely cited in Horatian studies.

title: Commentum in Horati Epistulas
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Pomponius Porphyrio's Commentum in Horati Epistulas is an important early commentary on Horace's Epistles, providing valuable grammatical, rhetorical, and historical explanations. Written in the 3rd century CE, it is a key witness to the ancient reception of Horace and the transmission of Horatian scholarship. Porphyrio's work is a specialist text, but commonly used by Horatian scholars and historians of classical philology.

title: Commentum in Horati Epodos
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Pomponius Porphyrio's 'Commentum in Horati Epodos' is a key late-antique commentary on Horace's Epodes, important for textual criticism and the history of Horatian reception. It is a standard reference for scholars of Horace and Latin scholia.

title: Commentum in Horati Sermones
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Pomponius Porphyrio's commentary on Horace's Sermones (Satires) is a key late-antique grammatical and exegetical source. It is essential for scholars of Horatian reception and ancient literary criticism.

title: Vita Horati
author: Pomponius Porphyrio
classification_notes: Pomponius Porphyrio's Vita Horati is a brief biographical sketch of the poet Horace, composed as part of a larger commentary tradition. Though modest in scope and word count, it preserves valuable ancient biographical information and supports the interpretation of Horace's works.

title: carmina
author: Porcius Licinus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Porcius Licinus fl. 2nd cent. B.C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Poetic fragments (carmina) of Porcius Licinus, an early Latin poet known for epic themes. His works survive only in brief quotations.

title: Precatio Omnium Herbarum
author: Precatio Omnium Herbarum
classification_notes: The Precatio Omnium Herbarum is a short prayer addressed to all herbs, possibly from a late antique magical or medical context. It is a rare text combining folk religion with botanical lore.

title: Precatio Terrae
author: Precatio Terrae
classification_notes: The Precatio Terrae is a brief anonymous Latin hymn or prayer to the Earth, surviving in a single manuscript. It is an obscure text, of interest primarily to specialists in Latin hymnody and religious poetry.

title: Priapea
author: Priapea
classification_notes: The Priapea is a collection of Latin poems (mainly epigrams) on the phallic god Priapus, of uncertain authorship and date. The poems are obscene and humorous, often attributed to various Augustan or Neronian poets. The collection is of moderate interest to classicists studying Roman sexuality, epigram, and Catullan tradition.

title: Sententiae
author: Pseudo-Varro
classification_notes: The 'Sententiae' attributed to Pseudo-Varro is a collection of moral sayings, likely compiled in late antiquity. It is of interest to specialists in the history of wisdom literature and the reception of Varro.

title: Epigrammata
author: Publilius Optatianus Porfyrius
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Porfyrius, P. Optianus fl. 325; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: These tiny epigrams by Optatianus Porfyrius, a late antique poet known for pattern poems, are marginal specimens of the epigrammatic tradition.

title: Sententiae
author: Publilius Syrus
classification_notes: The Sententiae of Publilius Syrus is a collection of moral maxims in verse, extracted from his mimes. Publilius was a writer of mimes active in the early Imperial period, and his Sententiae became a popular school text in antiquity. The collection survives in a manuscript tradition and offers a window into popular morality and the mime tradition. It is a specialist text read by students and scholars of Roman comedy and mime.

title: mimi
author: Publilius Syrus
classification_notes: Publilius Syrus was a writer of mimes in the late Republic. Only 19 words survive from his mimi (mime scripts). His sententiae (aphorisms) are better known. These dramatic fragments are too scant to reconstruct the mimes' plots, but they attest to a popular genre. The text is obscure.

title: versus Pupio attributi
author: Pupius (?)
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Pupius 1st cent. B.C; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: A few verses attributed to Pupius, a little-known epic poet of the first century BC; preserved only in fragments.

title: Historiae
author: Q. Aelius Tubero
classification_notes: Historiae by Q. Aelius Tubero survives only in fragments, providing insight into late Republican historiography.

title: liber ad C. Oppium, fr.
author: Q. Aelius Tubero
classification_notes: This solitary fragment of Aelius Tubero's letter to Oppius is among the smallest surviving Latin texts, preserving almost no content.

title: In Senatu Contra L. Pisonem
author: Q. Asconius Pedianus
classification_notes: Asconius's commentary on Cicero's speech 'In Senatu Contra L. Pisonem' is a vital scholarly work for understanding Ciceronian oratory and late Republican politics. Asconius provides historical and textual clarification, making this a commonly referenced text by students of Roman rhetoric and history.

title: In Toga Candida
author: Q. Asconius Pedianus
classification_notes: Asconius's commentary on Cicero's speech In Toga Candida is a valuable scholarly work that provides historical and rhetorical context. It is an important source for understanding Cicero's oratory and the politics of the late Republic.

title: Pro Cornelio
author: Q. Asconius Pedianus
classification_notes: Asconius's commentary on Cicero's Pro Cornelio is a valuable historical and rhetorical source, preserving details of the speech and its context.

title: Pro Milone
author: Q. Asconius Pedianus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Asconius Pedianus, Quintus 9 B.C.-76 A.D; perseus_editor=Colson, F. H.; perseus_year_published=1980; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Asconius's commentary on Cicero's speech Pro Milone, a key source for understanding the historical and rhetorical context of the trial. Asconius provides valuable scholia.

title: Pro Scauro
author: Q. Asconius Pedianus
classification_notes: Asconius's commentary on Cicero's 'Pro Scauro' is one of the few surviving ancient commentaries on Cicero's speeches. It provides historical and rhetorical background invaluable for modern scholarship.

title: carmina
author: Q. Aurelius Memmius Symmachus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Epic poetry, Latin; perseus_author=Symmachus, Q. Aurelius Memmius d. 525; perseus_editor=Morel, Willy; perseus_year_published=1927; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Only 150 words survive from the carmina of Q. Aurelius Memmius Symmachus, a late antique aristocrat and man of letters. These fragments, preserved in the Fragmenta Poetarum Latinorum, are too brief to assess literary quality but demonstrate continued poetic production in the 5th-6th century Roman cultural sphere. They are of interest to specialists in late antique Latin poetry and the transmission of fragmentary texts.

title: oratio
author: Q. Caecilius Metellus Macedonicus
source_metadata_summary: perseus_subject=Speeches, addresses, etc., Latin; perseus_author=Metellus Macedonicus, Q. Caecilius (Quintus Caecilius) approximately 188 B.C.-115 B.C; perseus_editor=Meyer, Heinrich; perseus_year_published=1842; perseus_language=Latin
classification_notes: Q. Caecilius Metellus Macedonicus' oratio survives only in a fragment. As an early Roman orator and statesman, his speech is of specialist interest for the history of Latin rhetoric and Republican politics.

title: orationes
author: Q. Caecilius Metellus Numidicus
classification_notes: The orationes of Q. Caecilius Metellus Numidicus survive only in a handful of fragments totaling 160 words. They are known primarily through quotations by later authors. These fragments provide scant evidence for the oratory of a prominent Late Republican statesman and are mainly of prosopographical interest.

title: Annales
author: Q. Claudius Quadrigarius

The work is commonly studied by specialists in Roman historiography and Late Republican history.

title: Pescennius Niger Aeli Spartiani
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: The biography of Pescennius Niger from the Historia Augusta is a late antique imperial biography, part of a collection known for its historical unreliability. It is of specialist interest to scholars of Roman imperial history and late antique literature.

title: Trebelli Pollionis Divus Claudius
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: The Historia Augusta's life of Claudius, attributed to Trebellius Pollio, is a problematic but essential source for the third-century Roman emperors, blending fact and fiction.

title: Trebelli Pollionis Gallieni Duo
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: The Scriptores Historiae Augustae Trebelli Pollionis Gallieni Duo is a portion of the Historia Augusta, a late antique collection of imperial biographies. This section covers the two Gallieni (Gallienus and his son). It is a key source for the 3rd century AD but is of disputed reliability. It is widely cited in Roman historical studies.

title: Trebelli Pollionis Tyranni Triginta
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: This biography of the Thirty Tyrants by Trebellius Pollio is a component of the Historia Augusta, a problematic but essential late antique collection of imperial biographies. Covering the numerous usurpers during the reign of Gallienus, it provides unique, if often unreliable, detail on a turbulent period of the 3rd century CE. The work is commonly read by specialists in Roman imperial history, though its fictionalized elements require careful source criticism.

title: Trebelli Pollionis Valeriani Duo
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: A biography of Valerian from the Historia Augusta, attributed to Trebellius Pollio. The work is a key but unreliable source for the third-century crisis, blending fact and fiction.

title: Vita Clodii Albini Iulii Capitolini
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: The Vita Clodii Albini, attributed to Iulius Capitolinus in the Historia Augusta, is a problematic and possibly fictionalized biography of the third-century emperor Clodius Albinus. It is a key source for the study of late Roman historiography and the contentious reliability of the Historia Augusta.

title: Vita Marci Antonini Philosophi Iuli Capitolini
author: Scriptores Historiae Augustae
classification_notes: This biography of Marcus Aurelius from the Historia Augusta, attributed to Iulius Capitolinus, is a key but controversial source for the life and philosophy of the emperor.

title: Rerum Gestarum Libri
author: Sempronius Asellio
classification_notes: Sempronius Asellio's historical work survives in fragments, an early annalistic history.

title: Controversiae
author: Seneca the Elder

classification_notes: Seneca the Elder's Controversiae, a collection of declamatory exercises and legal arguments. A key source for Roman rhetoric and education, offering insight into declamation and the judicial system.

classification_notes: Seneca the Elder's Controversiae excerpta are key for Roman rhetorical education.

classification_notes: These fragmenta of Seneca the Elder are minimal remains of his rhetorical works. They are of specialist interest for understanding Roman declamation.

classification_notes: Suasoriae are rhetorical exercises by Seneca the Elder, a key source for understanding Roman declamation. They are commonly studied in the context of Roman rhetorical education.

classification_notes: A fragmentary poem (carmen) by Septimius Serenus, a poet known for rustic or pastoral themes. The surviving 181 words, mainly from the De Rerum Natura-like context, are cited by ancient grammarians.

title: 1 Chronicles (1 Paraleipomena)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 1 Chronicles (1 Paraleipomena) from the Septuagint is a historical book covering genealogies and the reign of David. It is moderately read in biblical scholarship, though less central than the Pentateuch or Gospels. The text is of common interest in Old Testament studies.

title: 1 Esdras
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 1 Esdras, from the Septuagint in Latin, is a historical book covering the period from Josiah's reforms to the rebuilding of the Temple, parallel to Ezra-Nehemiah.

title: 1 Kings (3 Kingdoms)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 1 Kings (3 Kingdoms) from the Septuagint in Latin version narrates the history of the Israelite monarchy, including Solomon and the division of the kingdom. It is a key historical book of the Old Testament, widely used in Christian and Jewish tradition.

title: 1 Maccabees
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 1 Maccabees from the Septuagint is a historical book recounting the Maccabean revolt. It is a key source for Jewish history in the Hellenistic period and is widely read in biblical studies.

title: 1 Samuel (1 Kingdoms)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Latin version of 1 Samuel (1 Kingdoms) from the Septuagint tradition. This historical book is a cornerstone of the Old Testament and was widely transmitted in Latin through the Vetus Latina and Vulgate.

title: 2 Chronicles (2 Paraleipomena)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 2 Chronicles, also known as 2 Paraleipomena in the Septuagint, recounts the history of the Judahite monarchy and is here in a Latin translation.

title: 2 Esdras (Ezra and Nehemiah)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 2 Esdras (Ezra and Nehemiah) from the Septuagint in Latin translation is a key historical book of the Old Testament, recounting the return from exile and reconstruction of Jerusalem.

title: 2 Kings (4 Kingdoms)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 2 Kings (4 Kingdoms) from the Septuagint is a historical book of the Old Testament, covering the period of the divided monarchy. Its Latin version is commonly studied in biblical and historical scholarship.

title: 2 Maccabees
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 2 Maccabees from the Septuagint is a deuterocanonical historical book recounting the Maccabean revolt. It is commonly read in religious and historical scholarship, particularly for its theological and narrative content.

title: 2 Samuel (2 Kingdoms)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 2 Samuel (2 Kingdoms) from the Septuagint is a historical book of the Old Testament, detailing the reign of King David. Its Latin version is commonly studied in biblical and historical scholarship.

title: 3 Maccabees
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 3 Maccabees, a deuterocanonical book from the Septuagint, recounts the persecution of Jews under Ptolemy IV. Its Latin translation contributes to the biblical tradition.

title: 4 Maccabees
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: 4 Maccabees is a philosophical and religious work in the Septuagint. It is of specialist interest within Jewish and Christian literature.

title: Amos
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Amos is a prophetic book of the Hebrew Bible, attributed to the prophet Amos. It addresses social justice and divine judgment, and is a relatively short and specialized text within the biblical canon.

title: Baruch
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Baruch from the Latin Vulgate is a deuterocanonical book of the Old Testament. It is commonly read in religious and scholarly contexts, particularly for its historical and theological content.

title: Bel and the Dragon (Theodotion version)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Bel and the Dragon (Theodotion version) from the Septuagint is a deuterocanonical addition to Daniel. It is of interest for biblical textual criticism and the study of Hellenistic Jewish literature.

title: Canticles (Song of Solomon, Song of Songs)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Song of Songs (Canticles) from the Latin tradition, here represented under the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible) source. This poetic book of the Bible, celebrated for its erotic allegory of divine love, was widely read and commented upon in Christian and Jewish traditions. The Latin version, whether from the Vetus Latina or Vulgate, was enormously influential in medieval mysticism and exegesis. However, the metadata context of this row is unclear.

title: Daniel (Old Greek version)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Daniel in the Old Greek version (Septuagint), containing apocalyptic visions and the story of Daniel in Babylon. This Greek version differs from the later Masoretic text.

title: Daniel (Theodotion version)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Daniel (Theodotion version) is a deuterocanonical addition in the Septuagint. It is of specialist interest for biblical textual criticism.

title: Deuteronomy
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Deuteronomy, the fifth book of the Torah and the Christian Old Testament, is a canonical religious text. This entry presents the Septuagint (Greek) version of the book. It is a core text for the study of the Hebrew Bible in its Greek translation and for Judeo-Christian tradition.

title: Epistle of Jeremiah (Letter of Jeremiah)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Latin Epistle of Jeremiah is a deuterocanonical work warning against idolatry.

title: Esther
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Esther in the Septuagint is a narrative of the Jewish queen Esther who saves her people from persecution. The Septuagint version includes additional material not found in the Hebrew Bible. It is an important text for biblical studies, Jewish history, and the development of the canon.

title: Exodus
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Exodus from the Septuagint, in Latin translation, recounts the liberation of the Israelites from Egypt and the giving of the Law. As a foundational narrative of the Old Testament, it is a cornerstone of Judeo-Christian scripture and has shaped Latin religious and historical thought.

title: Ezekiel
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Ezekiel from the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible), a major prophetic book.

title: Genesis
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Genesis is the foundational book of the Bible.

title: Habakkuk
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Habakkuk from the Septuagint is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. This Greek translation is a key text for biblical studies and the history of Judaism and Christianity. It is a major work within its religious tradition.

title: Haggai
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Haggai in the Septuagint is a post-exilic prophetic book urging the rebuilding of the Temple. Its call to prioritize divine worship is studied in the context of restoration theology and the formation of the Hebrew Bible.

title: Hosea
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Hosea is a prophetic book of the Old Testament, using the metaphor of marital unfaithfulness to depict Israel's covenant relationship with God. It is a canonical text, widely studied in biblical theology and Latin patristic exegesis.

title: Isaiah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Isaiah in the Septuagint tradition, here in a Latin version, is a major prophetic book of profound theological and literary importance. Its messianic prophecies have shaped both Jewish and Christian thought.

title: Jeremiah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Septuagint version of the Book of Jeremiah, here presented in a Latin edition, is a major prophetic text of the Old Testament. Its translation from Greek into Latin reflects the complex textual history of the biblical canon.

title: Job
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Job from the Septuagint is a profound poetic and philosophical work of the Hebrew Bible. Its Latin versions are central to the scriptural canon and have been widely read and interpreted.

title: Joel
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Joel in the Septuagint, a short prophetic book containing the locus classicus for the outpouring of the Spirit (Joel 2:28–32). It is prominent in Christian liturgical use.

title: Jonah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Jonah from the Septuagint is a prophetic book with a narrative of repentance and divine mercy. It is widely read in Jewish and Christian traditions.

title: Joshua A (text of family A)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The book of Joshua A (text of family A) from the Septuagint is a version of the historical book of Joshua in the Old Greek Bible. In a Latin corpus, this entry likely represents a critical edition or manuscript variant. It is of specialist interest for Septuagint studies and textual criticism.

title: Joshua B (text of family B)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: This Latin version of Joshua, representing the family B recension of the Septuagint, is a key witness to the textual history of the Old Testament in translation. It is a substantial biblical book widely used in liturgical and scholarly contexts.

title: Judges A (text of family A)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Judges A, a text of the family A tradition from the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible). This textual tradition is a key witness to the history of the Septuagint and its Latin reception. The text is of major importance for biblical scholars and specialists in the transmission of the Old Testament. The Latin language tag may be a metadata artifact reflecting the source collection.

title: Judges B (text of family B)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Judges from the Septuagint in its B text tradition, here in Latin version. It is a historical book of the Old Testament covering the period of the judges. This version reflects an early Greek translation.

title: Judith
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Judith is a deuterocanonical book of the Bible, telling the story of a Jewish widow who saves her people. It is a work of historical fiction with religious and moral themes, less prominent than the major biblical books.

title: Lamentations
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Lamentations, from the Septuagint tradition, is a collection of poetic laments over the destruction of Jerusalem. It is a key biblical book for understanding Jewish and Christian responses to suffering and exile, and it has been widely used in liturgy and literature.

title: Leviticus
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Leviticus from the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible) is a major book of the Pentateuch. The Septuagint is the foundational Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible, central to biblical studies and early Christianity.

title: Malachi
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Malachi, from the Septuagint, is the last book of the Minor Prophets, addressing covenant faithfulness and the coming day of the Lord. It is a key prophetic text for biblical theology and eschatology.

title: Micah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Micah in the Septuagint is a prophetic book addressing social justice and the coming of a ruler from Bethlehem. Its influence on New Testament messianic expectations makes it an important text for biblical theology and intertestamental studies.

title: Nahum
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Latin version of the minor prophet Nahum, part of the Septuagint textual tradition. Though brief, it contributes to the Latin biblical witness.

title: Numbers
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Numbers in the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible), a key text of the Hebrew Bible translated into Greek and later into Latin. It contains census records, laws, and narratives from the wilderness period.

title: Obadiah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Obadiah from the Septuagint is a prophetic book of the Old Testament. This Greek translation is a common text within biblical studies.

title: Odes
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Odes is a book of the Septuagint containing lyric hymns. It is of specialist interest within biblical and liturgical studies.

title: Proverbs
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Proverbs from the Latin Vulgate is a wisdom book of the Old Testament, widely read for its moral and practical teachings. Its Latin version is a key text for Vulgate studies and biblical exegesis.

title: Psalms
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Psalms (Septuagint version), central to Jewish and Christian liturgy and devotion, are among the most widely read texts in the Latin tradition via the Vulgate. They have profoundly influenced music, poetry, and religious practice.

title: Psalms of Solomon
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Psalms of Solomon, a pseudepigraphical work from the Septuagint tradition, is important for Second Temple Jewish piety and messianism. Its Latin version is less known but studied in specialized contexts.

title: Qohelet (Ecclesiastes)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Ecclesiastes (Qohelet) in the Old Latin or Vulgate tradition. This philosophical meditation on the vanity of life has profoundly influenced religious thought and existential inquiry.

title: Ruth
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Ruth from the Septuagint, here in a Latin version, is a short biblical narrative of loyalty and redemption. It is a foundational religious text, widely studied in Christian tradition and influential in Latin literature through the Vulgate and patristic commentary.

title: Sirach (Ben Sira, Ecclesiasticus)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Sirach (Ecclesiasticus) in Latin, a wisdom book from the Septuagint tradition.

title: Susanna (Old Greek version)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Latin version of Susanna from the Old Greek (Septuagint) tradition. This narrative addition to Daniel is a distinctive piece of biblical literature.

title: Susanna (Theodotion version)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Susanna narrative from the Septuagint (Theodotion version) is a deuterocanonical addition to Daniel. It is a short story (1,048 words) about a virtuous woman falsely accused. It is of specialist interest for biblical apocrypha studies and Septuagint textual criticism.

title: TobitBA (text of family BA)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Book of Tobit in the Latin textual tradition (family BA), part of the broader Septuagint translation.

title: TobitS (text of Sinaiticus)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: Tobit (Sinaiticus version) is a deuterocanonical book recounting a story of faith, healing, and divine providence.

title: Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Wisdom of Solomon (Sapientia), from the Septuagint, is a deuterocanonical wisdom book attributed to Solomon, treating themes of divine wisdom, justice, and immortality. It is influential in Christian theology and literature.

title: Zechariah (Zachariah)
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The book of Zechariah (Zachariah) from the Septuagint (Old Greek Bible) is a prophetic text of the Hebrew Bible, preserved in Greek translation. In this Latin edition, it represents the Old Latin version before Jerome's Vulgate. It is moderately read in biblical and patristic scholarship, but less so than major prophets like Isaiah.

title: Zephaniah
author: Septuagint (Old Greek Bible)
classification_notes: The Latin version of the prophet Zephaniah, part of the Septuagint tradition, represents an important witness to the Old Latin biblical text. Though a minor prophetic book, it holds steady significance in biblical and patristic studies.

title: grammatica
author: Ser. Clodius

classification_notes: A brief fragment of Ser. Clodius' grammatical work. Clodius was a Roman grammarian known for his etymological interests, but the surviving text is too short for substantive analysis.

title: iurisprudentia
author: Ser. Sulpicius Rufus
classification_notes: Ser. Sulpicius Rufus was a leading Roman jurist of the late Republic. This fragmentary work, 'iurisprudentia,' represents his legal writings, which were highly influential in the development of Roman law. The text is of specialist interest, primarily studied within the context of Roman legal history and jurisprudence.

title: oratio
author: Ser. Sulpicius Rufus
classification_notes: These fragments of a speech (oratio) by Ser. Sulpicius Rufus, a prominent Roman jurist and orator, total only 54 words. The remains are too brief to provide meaningful insight into his oratory. The work is of minimal interest, except to specialists in fragmentary Roman rhetorical texts.

title: carmen
author: Sevius Nicanor

classification_notes: Sevius Nicanor's 'carmen' is a fragmentary poem, known only from a citation. The author is a minor figure, and the work is of interest only to specialists in Republican poetry.

title: grammatica
author: Sex. (vel Sp.) Ennius
classification_notes: Ennius' grammatica survives only in fragments. As an early Roman grammatical work, it is of interest to historians of Latin linguistics, but its brevity limits its readership.

title: De Agrorum Qualitate
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus' De Agrorum Qualitate, a technical treatise on land surveying and field classification. Part of the Roman gromatic (land measurement) tradition, it is essential for understanding Roman land management.

title: De Aquis Urbis Romae
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus's De Aquis Urbis Romae, a detailed report on the aqueducts of Rome, essential for Roman engineering and urban history.

title: De Arte Mensoria
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus's De Arte Mensoria, a technical work on land surveying; survives only in fragments.

title: De Controversiis
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus's De Controversiis deals with legal disputes arising from land surveying. It is a technical work within the corpus of the Roman agrimensores, important for understanding Roman property law and surveying.

title: De Limitibus
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus' De Limitibus is a technical treatise on land surveying and boundary marking, surviving in a fragmentary state. It is a specialist text of interest to historians of Roman surveying and technical literature.

title: Strategemata
author: Sex. Iulius Frontinus
classification_notes: Frontinus's 'Strategemata' is a collection of military stratagems, a technical manual for Roman military commanders. It is a specialist work, of interest to scholars of Roman military history and tactics.

title: carmen
